CZ_NAME: MOUNTAINS SOUTHWESTERN SHASTA COUNTY TO WESTERN COLUSA COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. Impacts from the relatively dry start to the calendar year have started to become evident across interior Northern California. Much of the region has increased the severity of the drought conditions with only the Delta remaining unchanged in drought level declaration for the month of March. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue with plans for increased restrictions forthcoming. Agricultural surface water deliveries are already scheduled to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of April, the U.S. Drought Monitor made little change to the zone with the majority of Zone 63 in extreme drought (D3) with the far western border of the zone remaining in severe drought (D2). While the zone saw above average precipitation for the month of April, the zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year at around 45-60% of average. Drought impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy continues to be impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable environment set up behind a cold front as the parent upper-level trough moved overhead and instability increased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Schoolhouse Peak RAWS measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 63 mph between 8:45 p.m. and 9 p.m.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved east across the Pacific Northwest on April 11.  This system spread precipitation southward into the area between midday on April 11 through the early morning of April 12 as a cold front dropped through the region.  The system also produced very strong wind gusts across Kern County with several stations measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph with a few low impact indicator sites measuring gusts exceeding 100 mph.  North of Kern County widespread gusts above 30 mph were observed, mainly during the cold frontal passage.  Precipitation was most significant over the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills north of Kings Canyon with several stations measuring between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of liquid precipitation with elevations above 6000 feet picking up between 3 and 8 inches of new snowfall.  Further south the precipitation was less significant with only a tenth to a quarter inch of liquid precipitation in the Sierra Nevada and rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley was minimal due to rain shadowing as areas north of Fresno generally picked up a tenth of an inch or less with little to no rainfall from Fresno southward.  The precipitation moved out of the area by late morning on April 12 and winds diminished during the evening as the system moved to the east of our area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blue Max RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 86 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low pressure system moved through the area between the late morning of April 21 and the early morning of April 22.  This system produced widespread precipitation across the area.  Much of the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills measured between 0.75 and 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation with the majority of the precipitation falling as snow above 6000 feet.  Several SNOTELs had an estimated 15 or more inches of snow in Yosemite Park and 6 to 14 inches further south.  Most of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and the Tehachapi Mountains measured between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of rainfall while rain shadowing limited the Kern County Deserts to a tenth of an inch or less.  However, a period of gusty downslope winds impacted the Kern County Mountains and Deserts between the afternoon of April 21 and the evening of April 22 with several stations measuring gusts above 45 mph and a few low impact indicator sites reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL reports of 6 to 12 inches of estimated new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry upper trough moved through central California during the early morning of April 28 bringing a much cooler airmass into the area.  This system also brought increased winds to the area during the afternoon and evening hours as pressure gradients strengthened.  Strong wind gusts impacted the mountains and deserts of Kern County during this time frame with the Mojave Desert slopes being the most impacted.  Several stationed reported wind gusts exceeding 45 mph with several gusts exceeding 60 mph being observed during the early evening.  The strong wind gusts also resulted in at least four semi-trucks being blown over in the Mojave area.  The winds slowly diminished overnight as the trough moved east of our area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mojave Airport AWOS measured a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. Much of the region has increased the severity of the drought conditions with only the Delta remaining unchanged in drought level declaration for the month of March. Agricultural surface water deliveries are already scheduled to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators.

Drought impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy continues to be significantly impacted.

Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue with plans for increased restrictions forthcoming. Agricultural surface water deliveries are already scheduled to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of April, the U.S. Drought Monitor degraded the southwestern portion of the zone to extreme drought (D3) with the remainder of the zone in severe drought (D2). While the zone saw well above average precipitation for the month of April, the zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year at around 70-95% of average. Drought impacts include but are not limited too: agriculture, water supplies, recreation, and ecosystems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper low pressure center dropped southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska on October 21 and moved into into the Pacific Northwest on October 22.  A strong cold front that was associated with this system pushed southward through central California during the afternoon and evening of October 22 which brought a period a strong winds to the south end of the Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains and Mojave Desert slopes in Kern County.  Several stations measured peak wind gusts exceeding 55 mph while a few low impact indicator sites recorded peak gusts exceeding 75 mph.  This system also brought a much cooler airmass to the area as temperatures across the area were well above normal during the first three weeks of the month were well above normal on most.  Temperatures finally cooled to near normal by the 22nd and to below normal levels behind the system on October 23.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cache Creek CHP station measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved east across the Pacific Northwest on April 11.  This system spread precipitation southward into the area between midday on April 11 through the early morning of April 12 as a cold front dropped through the region.  The system also produced very strong wind gusts across Kern County with several stations measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph with a few low impact indicator sites measuring gusts exceeding 100 mph.  North of Kern County widespread gusts above 30 mph were observed, mainly during the cold frontal passage.  Precipitation was most significant over the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills north of Kings Canyon with several stations measuring between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of liquid precipitation with elevations above 6000 feet picking up between 3 and 8 inches of new snowfall.  Further south the precipitation was less significant with only a tenth to a quarter inch of liquid precipitation in the Sierra Nevada and rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley was minimal due to rain shadowing as areas north of Fresno generally picked up a tenth of an inch or less with little to no rainfall from Fresno southward.  The precipitation moved out of the area by late morning on April 12 and winds diminished during the evening as the system moved to the east of our area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison Station at Grapevine Canyon Rd measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was slightly below normal across much of the area in April. This did not improve the long term precipitation deficit from the previous several months.  As a result, nearly all of the central California Interior remained in an extreme drought at the end of April, 2022.  As of April, 2022, most of the impacted area has received less than 40% of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South End of the Lower Sierra was classified as being in a extreme drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inside slider type low pressure system moving through the Great Basin brought gusty northerly winds up to 50 mph in the hills and very low relative humidity to interior parts of the SF Bay Area Thursday night into Friday afternoon. This combination of gusty winds and dry air led to near critical fire weather conditions in parts of the interior. A Wind Advisory was issued for this time frame for the interior North Bay Mountains, East Bay Hills and East Bay Interior Valleys. Report of a small brush fire was received during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vegetation fire at Highway 4 near Willow Pass Road in the City of Concord broke out just after 12:00 pm. Contra Costa County Fire responded when smoke was spotted. Strong winds quickly spread this fire to approximately 20 acres, before it was contained. No damage to structures or persons was reported, but three businesses in the area were briefly evacuated. https://eastcountytoday.net/contra-costa-county-firefighters-battling-vegetation-fire-along-willow-pass/.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol issued a travel advisory for I-580 in Alameda County due to strong winds.

This system brought a brief  period of significant precipitation north of Fresno County with several stations in the Sierra Nevada picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation.  Several SNOTEL stations above 7000 feet in Madera and Mariposa Counties picked up between 3 and 7 inches of new snowfall. Several stations measured gusts exceeding 45 mph with a some low impact indicator sites measured gusts exceeding 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds developed around the San Francisco Bay Area and Central Coast on the afternoon of April 11 after a cold front passed through the region earlier in the day. A wind advisory was issued for the entire CWA Sunday evening through 9 P.M. The region experienced widespread wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph, with localized gusts over 60 mph along the coast. The winds downed trees and powerlines across the area, leading to road closures, traffic snarls, and damage to houses and cars. Winds began to settle down later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In El Granada, Highway 1 was closed for several hours in both directions just south of Coronado St. because of downed communication lines, as per The San Mateo County Sheriff���s Office. https://sanfrancisco.cbslocal.com/2022/04/11/high-wind-topples-tree-downed-lines-palo-alto-south-san-francisco/.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. Impacts from the relatively dry start to the calendar year have started to become evident across interior Northern California. Much of the region has increased the severity of the drought conditions with only the Delta remaining unchanged in drought level declaration for the month of March. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue with plans for increased restrictions forthcoming. Agricultural surface water deliveries are already scheduled to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of April, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept all of Zone 15 in extreme drought. While the northeast portion of the zone saw above average precipitation for the month of April, the southwest part of the zone saw well below average. In general, the zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year at around 50-60% of average. Drought impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy continues to be significantly impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into northern California during the morning of April 16 spreading precipitation southward into central California. This system brought a brief  period of significant precipitation north of Fresno County with several stations in the Sierra Nevada picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation.  Several SNOTEL stations above 7000 feet in Madera and Mariposa Counties picked up between 3 and 7 inches of new snowfall.  Between a quarter and a half inch of rain fell in the San Joaquin valley north of Fresno County.  Further south, precipitation was much less significant with most stations in the Sierra Nevada picking up between one to two tenths of an inch of liquid precipitation and stations in the valley measuring less than a tenth of an inch of rainfall.  This system also produced some strong wind gusts over the mountains and deserts of Kern County between the late morning and late afternoon hours as the cold front that was associated with this system pushed through the area.  Several stations measured gusts exceeding 45 mph with a some low impact indicator sites measured gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bear Peak RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fourth round of lighter mountain rain/snow showers and gusty west-southwest winds accompanied this Pacific borne spring storm as it continued the unsettled pattern into the second half of April.  Strong west-southwest winds over the region brought the greatest impacts to the region as gusts exceeded  60 mph over some areas of western Nevada and affected highway driving conditions, area recreation, and local aviation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system moved across northern California on April 19.  This system did bring some light precipitation to the Yosemite Park area with several stations measuring a few hundredths of an inch of liquid precipitation.  Most of the precipitation from this system remained to the north of our area.  However, as this system moved across northern California; onshore gradients strengthened enough per a period of windy conditions across the mountains and deserts of Kern County during the afternoon and evening of April 19.  Several stations measured gusts exceeding 45 mph while a handful of stations had gusts exceeding 60 mph.  The winds decreased by the early morning hours of April 20 as the system moved east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. Impacts from the relatively dry start to the calendar year have started to become evident across interior Northern California. Much of the region has increased the severity of the drought conditions. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue with plans for increased restrictions forthcoming. Agricultural surface water deliveries are already scheduled to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. As of the month of May, the Public Affairs Officer at Bureau of Reclamation reported that they anticipate that in the Sacramento Valley alone, over 350,000 acres of farmland will be fallowed. It was reported that agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are both water-intensive industries and less water will mean communities across California are going to suffer this year during the drought, it���s just a question of how much more they suffer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of May, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept all of Zone 15 in extreme drought (D3). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year at around 50-60% of average. Water storage levels at Lake Shasta were reported at 40% of its total capacity, the lowest ever recorded at the beginning of May since record-keeping began in 1977. Drought impacts include but are not limited too: agriculture, water supplies, recreation, and ecosystems. Drought impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, earlier onset of irrigation, increased fire danger, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy continues to be significantly impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early September heat starting in August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SDAPL observation site in Apple Valley observed daily highs ranging from the upper 90s to upwards of 109 degrees through much of early September. The highest daily maximum temperature of 109 degrees occurred on the afternoons of Thursday, September 1st, Friday September 2nd, and Tuesday September 6th, with a high of 105 degrees occurring on both the 3rd of September. Highs on Friday, September 9th were significantly cooler due to clouds and rain associated with the approach of Hurricane Kay, which ended the heat wave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early September heat starting in August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SDOCL observation site in Ocotillo Wells observed daily highs ranging from 103 degrees to upwards of 116 degrees through much of early September. The highest daily maximum temperature of 116 degrees occurred on the afternoon of Tuesday September 6th, with a high of 115 degrees occurring on both the 5th of September. Highs on Friday, September 9th were significantly cooler due to clouds and rain associated with the approach of Hurricane Kay, which ended the heat wave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Border 32 Wildfire started on August 31, and continued burning until it was completely contained on September 5th. Total acres burned reached 4,456 acres and 10 structures were destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Border 32 fire started at 2:17 pm on August 31 at Barret Lake Road and Highway 94 near Barret Lake. Temperatures were as high as 108 degrees during firefighting efforts. Total acreage burned 4,456 acres, and many structures were destroyed, including three homes, three outbuildings, two sheds, two RVs, three barns and one commercial structure. Evacuation orders were issued. The fire was 100% contained by 4:55 PM PDT on September 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. Impacts from the relatively dry start to the calendar year have started to become evident across interior Northern California. Much of the region has increased the severity of the drought conditions. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue with plans for increased restrictions forthcoming. Agricultural surface water deliveries are already scheduled to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. As of the month of May, the Public Affairs Officer at Bureau of Reclamation reported that they anticipate that in the Sacramento Valley alone, over 350,000 acres of farmland will be fallowed. It was reported that agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are both water-intensive industries and less water will mean communities across California are going to suffer this year during the drought, it���s just a question of how much more they suffer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of May, the U.S. Drought Monitor degraded the southern portion of Zone 67 in Calaveras and Tuolumne counties to exceptional drought (D4), kept severe drought (D2) in the far northeastern portion of the zone as well as the remainder of the zone in extreme drought (D3). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year at around 60-90% of average. Drought impacts include but are not limited too: agriculture, water supplies, recreation, and ecosystems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper ridge pushed inland while a trough lingered over the Great basin before lifting out between the afternoon of May 22 and the morning of May 23.  The interaction between these features produced a strong pressure gradient which produced a period of gusty winds over portions of the mountains and deserts of Kern County.  Most noticeably along the Mojave Desert Slopes where several stations measured gusts exceeding 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modest swell brought some larger waves to the coast. This was not a large event, nor unusual.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shelter Cove Fire Department responded to a call about two hikers in the water who had tried to pass a point while hiking The Lost Coast Trail. The tides were not very low this day during the afternoon, nor particularly high, about 4 feet. The swell was around 11 feet at 10 seconds, which brought some larger waves to the Black Sands Beach, though well below advisory criteria.  Still, in this particular spot is a steep and dangerous beach. One of the hikers was pronounced dead when rescuers arrived, while the other was injured and rushed to the hospital (and did survive).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Coastal Fire began on May 11th, with the cause currently unknown. Up to 20 homes were destroyed as the fire burned into Laguna Niguel, each of which were valued between $2-5 million. Evacuation orders and care shelters were set up, with no injuries or fatalities reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coastal Fire ignited on May 11th around 2:50 PM and was contained on May 17th at 7:54 AM. The fire consumed approximately 200 acres and burned up to 20 homes, each of which were valued between $2-5 million, total estimated around $60 million. Evacuation Orders and Warnings were issued for zones within the City of Laguna Niguel. Approximately 900 homes were evacuated as a result. Cause currently unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty northwest winds developed behind a departing storm system on March 4th. Wind gusts reached 30 to 45 mph along much of the coast, with gusts 40 to 50  mph in the higher peaks. A wind advisory was issued from the morning through the evening for the entire coastline from North Bay through Big Sur, along with San Francisco and the Bay Shoreline. The strong winds led to downed powerlines, power outages, traffic blockages, and interruption to ferry service in SF Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Petaluma Police Department received multiple reports of downed power lines. One was located at Petaluma Boulevard South and Mountainview Avenue, where power was reportedly out. Southbound traffic was being diverted. The other downed line blocked traffic at St. Francis and Caulfield Lane and South McDowell and Caulfield. https://www.ktvu.com/news/wind-downs-power-lines-in-petaluma-ferries-to-tiburon-replaced-by-buses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Heritage Fire began on Monday, March 14th near Oro Grande. It grew to about 500 acres and crossed into the city limits of Victorville, CA on Tuesday March 15th. Though some structures were threatened, no damage directly from the fire was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Heritage Fire erupted on Monday March 14th, and burned nearly 500 acres. Gusty winds fueled the fire causing it to grow to 10-15 acres within 30 minutes of initiation. After approximately one hour, the fire had jumped to 80 acres and was threatening at least 20 structures. Evacuations were issued south of Melrose. The Southern California Logistics Airport (KVCV) reported west wind gusts around 15 mph, and relative humidity of around 12 percent.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a period of unseasonably warm temperatures across the area between March 22 and March 26, an upper low pressure system approached the southern California coast on March 27 and brought increased winds to the West Side Hills and the Tehachapi Mountains during the evening of March 27 through the morning of March 28. Several stations reported gusts exceeding 45 mph with a few stations measuring gusts above 55 mph. As the low moved inland on March 28, a band of precipitation pushed eastward across the area during the morning and afternoon hours. Behind this band, skies cleared out and with cooler air pushing into the area aloft, instability was sufficient for thunderstorms to develop across the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills during the afternoon through the early evening hours although the thunderstorms were rapid movers and did not produce any significant flooding as a result. Most of the San Joaquin valley and Sierra Foothills picked up between a quarter inch and a half inch of rainfall with locations impacted by thunderstorms picking up more.  Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 0.40 and 0.80 inches of liquid precipitation. Many stations above 5500 feet in the Sierra Nevada picked up between 3 and 8 inches of new snowfall while many stations above 5500 feet in the Tehachapi Mountains picked up between 2 and 6 inches of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Joaquin Ridge measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two closed upper low pressure systems produced widespread precipitation, high-elevation snow and lightning from March 3 to 6. The first system occurred on March 3, producing strong winds across the mountains and deserts, scattered thunderstorms, and light snow in the mountains. The second, colder system, occurred March 5 and 6, and produced heavier snow in the San Bernardino and San Diego County Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Highways reported 2 inches of snow. Multiple reports of cars stuck in snow on Route 330. Chain control in effect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level low impacted Southern California on March 28th and 29th. Widespread precipitation occurred with this system, with areas of heavy rain at lower elevations and snow in the mountains. Heavy rain in spots led to flooding, which then led to some water rescues, road closures, as well as a small mud and debris flow on the Bond Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain began on the morning of March 28th and continued through the afternoon on March 29th. Rainfall of 0.75 to 1.50 inches fell across much of coastal and valley locations in San Diego County, with far southern portions of San Diego County as well as the foothills and mountains receiving 1.50 to 2.00 inches.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was closer to normal in March.  However, due to the extreme dry period in January and February and a long term precipitation deficit from the previous 18 months, nearly all of the central California Interior was classified as being in an extreme drought by the end of March, 2022.  As of March, 2022, most of the impacted area has received less than 40% of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Side Mountains north of Highway 198 were classified as being in a extreme drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a period of unseasonably warm temperatures across the area between March 22 and March 26, an upper low pressure system approached the southern California coast on March 27 and brought increased winds to the West Side Hills and the Tehachapi Mountains during the evening of March 27 through the morning of March 28. Several stations reported gusts exceeding 45 mph with a few stations measuring gusts above 55 mph. As the low moved inland on March 28, a band of precipitation pushed eastward across the area during the morning and afternoon hours. Behind this band, skies cleared out and with cooler air pushing into the area aloft, instability was sufficient for thunderstorms to develop across the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills during the afternoon through the early evening hours although the thunderstorms were rapid movers and did not produce any significant flooding as a result. Most of the San Joaquin valley and Sierra Foothills picked up between a quarter inch and a half inch of rainfall with locations impacted by thunderstorms picking up more.  Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 0.40 and 0.80 inches of liquid precipitation. Many stations above 5500 feet in the Sierra Nevada picked up between 3 and 8 inches of new snowfall while many stations above 5500 feet in the Tehachapi Mountains picked up between 2 and 6 inches of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL reports of 3 to 8 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Jim Wildfire was ignited on March 2, 2022 and was declared out on March 27. This wildfire consumed 500 acres of vegetation. The US Forest Service investigators determined the cause was unintentional, and was ignited during a Forest Service-sponsored wildlife and habit restoration maintenance project during barrier removal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jim Wildfire occurred on the Trabuco Ranger District in the Holy Jim Canyon area. This fire was unintentionally ignited during a wildlife and habitat restoration maintenance project. No structures were destroyed, but three minor injuries to firefighters were reported. The fire started in a drainage bottom and spread uphill and burned 553 acres of vegetation (chaparral and brush).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The storm that a quarter to a half inch of rain to much of the San Joaquin Valley during the previous day moved east the area overnight.  As a result, winds diminished and skies cleared out in some locations.  This allowed for patchy dense fog to form around sunrise in the Merced area and also east of Fresno and in Sanger. The fog briefly impacted travel along portions of SR 99 and SR 180 until dissipating within an hour after sunrise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Merced and Atwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and very wet atmospheric storm brought extended periods of moderate to heavy rain and periods of strong winds to much of the region, along with heavy high-elevation snow. Precipitation totals were around 1-3 inches for the Central Valley, 3-6 inches in the foothills, and 5-8 inches of liquid equivalent in the mountains. The rain brought widespread flooding to the region. This included significant river flooding on the Cosumnes River around Wilton due to multiple levee breaks, resulting in the area being evacuated and area highways and roads being closed, including SR-99. River flooding was also reported along the Mokelumne River near Benson's Ferry and Mormon Slough at Bellota. Flooding of streams, in low areas and from clogged storm drains also closed many other roads across Northern California, with several small communities being evacuated. Heavy snow also disrupted mountain travel, with multiple spinouts causing I-80 to be shut down for 18 hours. Highway 50 was closed in El Dorado County due to a combination of flooding and snow. High winds gusting 50-65 mph in the Central Valley caused large numbers of trees to fall, bringing widespread power failures across the area, with hundreds of thousands of customers impacted. Many local roads were closed to downed trees blocking them. Trees were also reported to have fallen on homes and automobiles. The storm with flooding continued into January, with several fatalities reported due to drowning and wind-downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The atmospheric river brought some heavy snow to high elevations, generally above 7,000 feet, with lighter accumulations down to 6,000 feet. Dodge Ridge Ski Resort measured 17 inches of new snow, Kirkwood 20 inches, Sierra at Tahoe 16 inches, Sugar Bowl 15 inches. Snow with some gusty winds brought lowered visibility as well as delays and chain controls at the Sierra passes, including Donner Pass on I-80 and Echo Summit on US Highway 50.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed ahead of an upper trough deepening off the Pacific Coast brought another round of strong winds area-wide, along with a chance for light showers and cooler temperatures to the region. Winds were the primary impact as thermal gradients tightened along the cold front with an accompanying increase in upper winds from a strong 100+ knot jet core that lifted north-northeast over the region. This setup increased confidence for moderate travel, recreation, and even fire weather impacts over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (HVRTM), Blue Streak weather station located 1.0 mi NNW Heavenly Ski Summit Mount Rose area reported a peak wind gust of 79 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. Impacts from the relatively dry start to the calendar year have started to become evident across interior Northern California. Much of the region has increased the severity of the drought conditions with only the Delta remaining unchanged in drought level declaration for the month of March. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue with plans for increased restrictions forthcoming. Agricultural surface water deliveries are already scheduled to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of March, the U.S. Drought Monitor degraded approximately the western half of the zone to extreme drought and kept the remainder of Zone 14 in severe drought. The zone saw emerging drought impacts due to the below average precipitation and relatively dry conditions the first few months of the calendar year. These impacts are similar to the previous year. However, reported impacts are more pronounced this March.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of March, the U.S. Drought Monitor degraded all of Zone 15 to extreme drought. The zone saw emerging drought impacts due to the below average precipitation and relatively dry conditions the first few months of the calendar year. These impacts are similar to the previous year. However, reported impacts are more pronounced this March. Impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage by as much as 85% without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels due to hay production elimination, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy has been significantly impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 1030 mb high over north-central Nevada and developing low pressure over northwestern Mexico led to Santa Ana winds occurring across Southern California. This event saw several gusts of 40-55 mph occur below mountain passes, with a few significant gusts over peaks and ridgelines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Santa Ana winds impacted the San Diego County Mountains on March 10th and 11th, with widespread wind gusts of 45-55 mph occurring, with some locally higher gusts as well. The Sill Hill observation site recorded a peak gust of 64 mph at 8:00 AM on the 11th, with another site near Crestwood measuring a peak gust of 57 mph at 9:50 AM on the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was closer to normal in March.  However, due to the extreme dry period in January and February and a long term precipitation deficit from the previous 18 months, nearly all of the central California Interior was classified as being in an extreme drought by the end of March, 2022.  As of March, 2022, most of the impacted area has received less than 40% of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mojave Desert was classified as being in a extreme drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system dropped southward off the California coast on March 3 then turned inland and moved across southern California during the morning of March 4 spreading moderate to locally heavy precipitation into the south San Joaquin Valley with localized street flooding being observed in the Bakersfield area. Light precipitation impacted the Tehachapi Mountains with the snow level lowering from 5000 feet at the onset of the precipitation to 4000 feet by the evening of March 4.  A colder system then dropped southward along the California Nevada border on March 5 which pushed a cold front southward through the area bringing mainly light precipitation to much of our area and lowering the snow level to around 2000 feet.  Snowfall was generally 3 to 6 inches of above 5000 feet and 1 to 3 inches between 2500 feet and 5000 feet.  The snow did result in traffic being paced along Interstate 5 over the Tejon Pass for a few hours on March 5 and briefly closed a few roads in the Kern County Mountains as some precautionary measures were taken.  Parts of the south San Joaquin Valley picked up between 0.75 inches and 1.5 inches of rainfall, but further north the the central valley most locations picked up between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported that the intersection of SR 184 and Redbank Rd. in Bakersfield was completely flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 28 and 31 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. Impacts from the relatively dry start to the calendar year have started to become evident across interior Northern California. Much of the region has increased the severity of the drought conditions with only the Delta remaining unchanged in drought level declaration for the month of March. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue with plans for increased restrictions forthcoming. Agricultural surface water deliveries are already scheduled to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of March, the U.S. Drought Monitor degraded the northern and western orders of Zone 66 to extreme drought and kept the remainder of the zone in severe drought. The zone saw emerging drought impacts due to the below average precipitation and relatively dry conditions the first few months of the calendar year. These impacts are similar to the previous year. However, reported impacts are more pronounced this March. Impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage by as much as 85% without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels due to hay production elimination, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, early weaning and shipping of cattle to summer range, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy has been significantly impacted.

However, reported impacts are more pronounced this March. Some of the impacts the region saw included; reduced forage, livestock stress, selling of livestock, decrease in water allocation, and hauling of water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A crash occurred around 1 AM in the University City area of San Diego. Just before the crash at 12:55 AM, the Miramar MCAS ASOS KNKX (2-3 SM NE of crash) reported light rain and mist with a visibility of 2.5 miles. Light rain turned heavier after 1 AM with reduced visibility as low as 1.5 miles. All southbound lanes of I-805 were shut down from 1 AM - 8 AM, when two lanes were reopened to southbound drivers. Driver appeared to avoid serious injury but the truck burst into flames and firefighters were dispatched.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a period of unseasonably warm temperatures across the area between March 22 and March 26, an upper low pressure system approached the southern California coast on March 27 and brought increased winds to the West Side Hills and the Tehachapi Mountains during the evening of March 27 through the morning of March 28. Several stations reported gusts exceeding 45 mph with a few stations measuring gusts above 55 mph. As the low moved inland on March 28, a band of precipitation pushed eastward across the area during the morning and afternoon hours. Behind this band, skies cleared out and with cooler air pushing into the area aloft, instability was sufficient for thunderstorms to develop across the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills during the afternoon through the early evening hours although the thunderstorms were rapid movers and did not produce any significant flooding as a result. Most of the San Joaquin valley and Sierra Foothills picked up between a quarter inch and a half inch of rainfall with locations impacted by thunderstorms picking up more.  Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 0.40 and 0.80 inches of liquid precipitation. Many stations above 5500 feet in the Sierra Nevada picked up between 3 and 8 inches of new snowfall while many stations above 5500 feet in the Tehachapi Mountains picked up between 2 and 6 inches of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Panoche Road RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 74 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station Yucc1 Yucaipa Ridge reported 2.28 inch of heavy rain from July 30 - August 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Border 32 Wildfire started on August 31. Total acres burned reached 4,456 acres and 10 structures were destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Border 32 fire started at 2:17 pm on August 31 at Barret Lake Road and Highway 94 near Barret Lake. Temperatures were as high as 108 degrees during firefighting efforts. Total acreage burned 4,456 acres, and many structures were destroyed, including three homes, three outbuildings, two sheds, two RVs, three barns and one commercial structure. Evacuation orders were issued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A record-breaking and prolonged heat wave started at the end of August and persisted through the week of Labor Day. Numerous records were broken and multiple wildfires were started as the result of extreme temperatures and drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idyllwild reached a high temperature of 96 degrees on August 31, breaking the previous record of 94 set in 2017.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A record-breaking and prolonged heat wave started at the end of August and persisted through the week of Labor Day. Numerous records were broken and multiple wildfires were started as the result of extreme temperatures and drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anaheim broke their daily high temperature record, reaching 106 degrees, breaking the previous record of 102 set in 2007. This also breaks the all-time monthly high temperature for the month of August, breaking the previous record of 105 degrees set on August 18, 2020.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A record-breaking and prolonged heat wave started at the end of August and persisted through the week of Labor Day. Numerous records were broken and multiple wildfires were started as the result of extreme temperatures and drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Campo tied the daily high temperature record, reaching 105 degrees, tying the previous record of 105 degrees set in 1998.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A record-breaking and prolonged heat wave started at the end of August and persisted through the week of Labor Day. Numerous records were broken and multiple wildfires were started as the result of extreme temperatures and drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous pre-hospital related calls and transports were recorded through the duration of the heatwave. Six calls were received on August 30, with age range of 7-79 years, with most seeing mild symptoms; many were outside recreating or working. On August 31, 13 calls were placed with the age range of 18-86 years, with 11 experiencing mild symptoms, 1 seeing moderate symptoms and 1 acute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level tropical moisture from Hurricane Frank which was situated off the Baja coast spread northward into southern and central California on August 1.  This moisture interacted with a very warm and unstable airmass which was present over the region at the time.  Showers and thunderstorms broke out during the afternoon and brought moderate to locally heavy rainfall to the mountains and deserts of eastern Kern County as well as to the Sierra Nevada.  The heavier thunderstorms prompted the issuance of two Flash Flood Warnings, two Flood Advisories and three Significant Weather Advisories.  There were reports of nuisance street flooding as a result of the heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media report of street flooding in downtown Tehachapi as the result of heavy rainfall.

Showers and thunderstorms broke out during the afternoon and brought moderate to locally heavy rainfall to the mountains and deserts of eastern Kern County as well as to the Sierra Nevada. EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported that the intersection of SR 14 and Jawbone Canyon Rd. was completely flooded over as a result of heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and very wet atmospheric storm brought extended periods of moderate to heavy rain and periods of strong winds to much of the region, along with heavy high-elevation snow. Precipitation totals were around 1-3 inches for the Central Valley, 3-6 inches in the foothills, and 5-8 inches of liquid equivalent in the mountains. The rain brought widespread flooding to the region. This included significant river flooding on the Cosumnes River around Wilton due to multiple levee breaks, resulting in the area being evacuated and area highways and roads being closed, including SR-99. River flooding was also reported along the Mokelumne River near Benson's Ferry and Mormon Slough at Bellota. Flooding of streams, in low areas and from clogged storm drains also closed many other roads across Northern California, with several small communities being evacuated. Heavy snow also disrupted mountain travel, with multiple spinouts causing I-80 to be shut down for 18 hours. Highway 50 was closed in El Dorado County due to a combination of flooding and snow. High winds gusting 50-65 mph in the Central Valley caused large numbers of trees to fall, bringing widespread power failures across the area, with hundreds of thousands of customers impacted. Many local roads were closed to downed trees blocking them. Trees were also reported to have fallen on homes and automobiles. The storm with flooding continued into January, with several fatalities reported due to drowning and wind-downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported significant flooding in the #1 lane of the ramp off I-5 due to a clogged storm drain. Some rainfall totals over 24 hours were 1.26 inches at Mather, 1.46 inches at Fair Oaks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of deep moisture moved over central California from the southeast during the afternoon of August 4. This moisture interacted with a hot and unstable airmass which was in place over the area resulting in the development of numerous showers and scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon over the Sierra Nevada, the adjacent Sierra foothills and the mountains and deserts of eastern Kern County.  Some of the thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall with amounts between a half inch and an inch of rainfall in a one hour period which prompted the issuance of thirteen Flash Flood Warnings and four Significant Weather Advisories during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sugarloaf RAWS measured 1.55 inches of rainfall from a thunderstorm in two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a strong cold frontal passage the morning on the morning of the 22nd, gusty northwesterly winds developed, bringing down some trees. The gustiest winds developed along the coast, where a wind advisory was issued from North Bay through the Big Sur coast from the late morning through the evening. Wind gusts reached 35 to 48 mph along the coast. Gale warnings were also issued for the coastal waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both directions on Rte-35 just south of Ware Road closed due to a tree on the roadway.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central California remained abnormally dry in August 2022. Scattered showers and thunderstorms did impact the area between the 1st and the 5th with some flooding being reported on the 1st and the 4th. Otherwise dry weather prevailed across the area. By the end of the month, nearly all of our area remained in an extreme or exceptional drought as most of our area has received less than a third of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Side Mountains north of Highway 198 were classified as being in an exceptional drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and very wet atmospheric storm brought extended periods of moderate to heavy rain and periods of strong winds to much of the region, along with heavy high-elevation snow. Precipitation totals were around 1-3 inches for the Central Valley, 3-6 inches in the foothills, and 5-8 inches of liquid equivalent in the mountains. The rain brought widespread flooding to the region. This included significant river flooding on the Cosumnes River around Wilton due to multiple levee breaks, resulting in the area being evacuated and area highways and roads being closed, including SR-99. River flooding was also reported along the Mokelumne River near Benson's Ferry and Mormon Slough at Bellota. Flooding of streams, in low areas and from clogged storm drains also closed many other roads across Northern California, with several small communities being evacuated. Heavy snow also disrupted mountain travel, with multiple spinouts causing I-80 to be shut down for 18 hours. Highway 50 was closed in El Dorado County due to a combination of flooding and snow. High winds gusting 50-65 mph in the Central Valley caused large numbers of trees to fall, bringing widespread power failures across the area, with hundreds of thousands of customers impacted. Many local roads were closed to downed trees blocking them. Trees were also reported to have fallen on homes and automobiles. The storm with flooding continued into January, with several fatalities reported due to drowning and wind-downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported vehicles unable to pass due to flooded roads at Cold Springs Rd and Richard Ave.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure which prevailed over the area between August 16 and August 20 provided for much warmer than normal temperatures across the area with dangerous heat prevailing during the daytime across the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills, lower Sierra foothills and the Kern River Valley.  An upper trough pushed into the Pacific Northwest on August 21 which weakened the large high pressure center and brought a cooling trend to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 102 and 106 degrees each day between August 16 and August 20.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong and gusty Santa Ana winds impacted the mountains of Los Angeles county. Some peak northeasterly wind gusts from the local mesonet include: Magic Mountain Truck Trail (gust 102 MPH), Warm Springs RAWS (gust 69 MPH) and Chilao RAWS (gust 61 MPH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain moved through an area near the McKinney wildfire in the evening.  Rainfall was estimated to be between 1 and 3 inches in an hour. This created a debris flow down Humbug Creek, on the eastern edge of the fire.  Four vehicles were reported swept off a culvert over Humbug Creek, and videos showed the swiftly rising water in the area.  One person had to be rescued from a partially submerged vehicle.  He sustained injuries, but survived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure which prevailed over the area between August 16 and August 20 provided for much warmer than normal temperatures across the area with dangerous heat prevailing during the daytime across the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills, lower Sierra foothills and the Kern River Valley.  An upper trough pushed into the Pacific Northwest on August 21 which weakened the large high pressure center and brought a cooling trend to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 104 and 108 degrees each day between August 16 and August 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Bronco Fire ignited on August 17th, and burned roughly 25 acres. It occurred very close to I-15, and actually necessitated the closure of three northbound lanes on I-15. This resulted in major traffic delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bronco Fire started near Matthews Rancho Road about 2 miles northwest of Devore, CA, and burned roughly 25 acres. The fire caused the closure of 3 northbound lanes on I-15 in the Cajon Pass, resulting in heavy traffic delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The McKinney Wildfire started around 1415 PDT on 07/29/22.  The cause remains under investigation.  Initially, it spread slowly, but winds from nearby thunderstorms caused it to spread rapidly on 07/30/22. As of 0530 PST on 09/01/22, the fire covered 60138 acres and was 99% contained. 185 structures were lost, and 73.7 million had been spent on firefighting efforts. Four people were killed by the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central California remained abnormally dry in August 2022. Scattered showers and thunderstorms did impact the area between the 1st and the 5th with some flooding being reported on the 1st and the 4th. Otherwise dry weather prevailed across the area. By the end of the month, nearly all of our area remained in an extreme or exceptional drought as most of our area has received less than a third of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Visalia, Porterville and Reedley areas were classified as being in an exceptional drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture surged northward into Northern California and a persistent low offshore brought the threat for thunderstorms the first several days of August. The 2nd of August was the peak for the thunder. A handful of special weather statements were issued over the course of several days. One storm set up over the River Complex Burn Scar from 2021 on August 2nd shortly before 6 p.m. A Flash Flood Warning was issued and a debris flow did occur on Coffee Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm set up over the a portion of the 2021 River Complex Burn Scar and caused a debris flow as reported by Trinity County OES on Coffee Creek on 2 August. The debris flow caused Coffee Creek Road to be unpassable. A Flash Flood Warning was issued shortly after 03/0110Z.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist (PWATs 2.0+ inches) and unstable (MLCAPE 2000-3000 J/KG) air mass led to be a very conducive environment for vigorous thunderstorm activity across portions of southeast California. Thunderstorms first developed across the higher terrain near Joshua Tree National Park as well as across the mountains of Northern Baja California. With the upper-level flow from the west, these thunderstorms moved eastward into Imperial and Riverside counties, moving into the very moist and unstable air mass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SR-77 closed in both directions between I-10 and SR-62 due to flooding in multiple locations. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought widespread rain, heavy mountain snow, gusty winds and mountain travel impacts to interior Northern California to start the month of December. Interstate 80 was closed in the Sierra for over 3 hours, and multiple spinouts were reported leading to periodic closures of Highway 50 at Echo Summit. Ski resorts reported multiple feet of snow with this storm between 11/30/22 and 12/02/22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported the closure of Interstate 80 due to heavy snow, as well as the periodic closures of Highway 50 at Echo Summit due to multiple spinouts. Ski resorts reported multiple feet of snow for this event. Dodge Ridge Ski Area reported 24 inches, Sierra-at-Tahoe reported 43 inches, Kirkwood reported 20 inches, and Sugar Bowl reported 17 inches of snow. Central Sierra Snow Lab reported 17 inches, and Caltrans reported 26 inches at Castle Peak over 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure expanded westward over California, bringing unseasonably hot temperatures to interior areas. Temperatures peaked in the 100s up to 110 deg on August 16. A heat advisory was issued for the late morning through evening hours covering all interior mountain zones, the southern Salinas Valley, and the East Bay interior valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Livermore Coop site reached a daily max record of 107 degrees, besting the previous record of 105 deg set in 1951. Other cities in the zone reached the low to mid 100s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonal cold upper-level trough and associated front moved through Northwest California. The system brought lower snow levels and post-frontal hail showers. Small hail covered roadways caused a couple of accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reports 4 inches of snow fell during a 7-hour duration at an elevation of 2,700 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another weekend storm delivered widespread rain, mountain snow and gusty winds to interior NorCal. Localized flooding, downed trees, and mountain travel impacts were observed starting on 12/9/22 through early 12/12/22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding reported at Montpellier Road and Whitmore Road near Foster Farms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm brought widespread rain, heavy mountain snow, gusty winds and mountain travel impacts to interior Northern California to start the month of December. Interstate 80 was closed in the Sierra for over 3 hours, and multiple spinouts were reported leading to periodic closures of Highway 50 at Echo Summit. Ski resorts reported multiple feet of snow with this storm between 11/30/22 and 12/02/22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 3 to 18 inches of snow were reported across the Burney Basin area according to the National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center over 48 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 3 to 18 inches of heavy snow fell across the Shasta County mountain areas over 48 hours, according to the National Operational Hydrologic Remote Sensing Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modest swell brought some larger waves to the coast. This was not a large event, nor unusual.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shelter Cove Fire Department responded to a call about two hikers in the water who had tried to pass a point while hiking The Lost Coast Trail. The tides were not very low this day during the afternoon, nor particularly high, about 4 feet. The swell was around 11 feet at 10 seconds, which brought some larger waves to the Black Sands Beach, though well below advisory criteria. Still, in this particular spot is a steep and dangerous beach. Witnesses described one large wave that washed up farther than any other recent wave causing a hiker to be washed into the rocks. One of the hikers was pronounced dead when rescuers arrived, while the other was injured during an attempted rescue and rushed to the hospital (and did survive).

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low pressure system dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska to off the Pacific Northwest coast on November 6. This system remained near the Pacific Northwest coast on November 7 and 8 before moving inland across Oregon during the morning of November 9 and into the intermountain west region later that day. A large trough associated with this system pushed into central California on November 7 and spread widespread precipitation into the area which continued until the morning of November 9. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELs above 6000 feet picking up between 20 and 30 inches of new snowfall. The Sierra foothills picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of rainfall while most of the San Joaquin Valley received a half inch to an inch of rain while the much of Kern County Deserts had a quarter to half an inch of rainfall. The system also brought strong southerly winds most noticeably to the higher elevations of Kern County on November 8. Several stations in the mountains and deserts area of Kern County reported wind gusts exceeding 50 mph during the late morning and early afternoon of November 8.  A few stations above Tejon Pass measured gusts exceeding 75 mph. Scattered thunderstorms developed over the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and November 8 with a few of them moving into the Sierra foothills.  Some of the thunderstorm produced locally heavy rainfall and strong outflow winds in addition to quarter to half inch diameter hail. Several thousand customers in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills lost power during the afternoon of November 8 either due to the strong winds, wet roadways causing accidents or thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bird Springs Pass RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high pressure center over the Great Basin provided for an easterly flow aloft over central California which allowed form enough mid/upper moisture to stream into the area for showers and thunderstorms to break out during the afternoon and evening. While several stations across the central California interior picked up between a hundredth of an inch and two tenths of an inch of rainfall, a few mountain locations that were impacted by heavier thunderstorms picked up more.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blue Max RAWS measured 1.13 inches of rainfall from a heavy thunderstorm over a two and a half hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. No changes were made to drought severity levels for the month of July. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with a portion of the Sierra Nevada in Tuolumne and Calaveras counties in exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and less water will mean communities across California are going to continue to see broad reaching impacts this year during the drought. Sierra Pacific Industries, owner of more than 1.7 million acres of timberland in California, closed its California forestlands to the public on July 1 due to extreme drought conditions and increasing risk of wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of July, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept all of Zone 13 in extreme drought (D3). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year at around 60% of average. Drought impacts include but are not limited too: agriculture, water supplies, recreation, and ecosystems. Sierra Pacific Industries closed public recreation excess to its forestlands on July 1 due to extreme drought conditions and increasing risk of wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. No changes were made to drought severity levels for the month of July. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with a portion of the Sierra Nevada in Tuolumne and Calaveras counties in exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and less water will mean communities across California are going to continue to see broad reaching impacts this year during the drought. Sierra Pacific Industries, owner of more than 1.7 million acres of timberland in California, closed its California forestlands to the public on July 1 due to extreme drought conditions and increasing risk of wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of July, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept approximately the western half of Zone 14 in extreme drought (D3) with the remainder of the zone in severe drought (D2). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year at around 60-85% of average. Drought impacts include but are not limited too: agriculture, water supplies, recreation, and ecosystems.  Sierra Pacific Industries closed public recreation excess to its forestlands on July 1 due to extreme drought conditions and increasing risk of wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second much stronger and colder early winter storm system brought more heavy snowfall to the Sierra late Sunday night through midweek.  A much weaker low-pressure system preceded this main event winter storm Saturday through Sunday.  A couple of waves of moderate to heavy snowfall followed and brought travel impacts to region passes through Tuesday. Lower-elevation snow showers then moved into the region Monday morning and became more widespread through Tuesday. Snow showers continued through Wednesday as the central low ejected further east across the Great Basin bringing only minor accumulations to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH01) located 0.9 mi S of Mammoth Mountain, CA, reported a peak wind gust of 70 mph.

A large trough associated with this system pushed into central California on November 7 and spread widespread precipitation into the area which continued until the morning of November 9. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELs above 6000 feet picking up between 20 and 30 inches of new snowfall. The Sierra foothills picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of rainfall while most of the San Joaquin Valley received a half inch to an inch of rain while the much of Kern County Deserts had a quarter to half an inch of rainfall. The system also brought strong southerly winds most noticeably to the higher elevations of Kern County on November 8. Several stations in the mountains and deserts area of Kern County reported wind gusts exceeding 50 mph during the late morning and early afternoon of November 8.  A few stations above Tejon Pass measured gusts exceeding 75 mph. Scattered thunderstorms developed over the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and November 8 with a few of them moving into the Sierra foothills.  Some of the thunderstorm produced locally heavy rainfall and strong outflow winds in addition to quarter to half inch diameter hail. Several thousand customers in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills lost power during the afternoon of November 8 either due to the strong winds, wet roadways causing accidents or thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Wells Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 69 mph.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central California remained abnormally dry in July 2022. Scattered showers and thunderstorms did impact the area between the 26th and the 31st. Otherwise dry weather prevailed across the area. By the end of the month, nearly all of our area remained in an extreme or exceptional drought as most of our area has received less than a third of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Side Mountains north of Highway 198 were classified as being in an exceptional drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Electra Fire began when weather conditions were moderately dry with relative humidity values around 47-49% and temperatures in the upper 70s. West winds 6 to 12 mph with gusts 15 to 20 mph were observed. High temperatures, low humidity, and steep, rugged terrain made containment of the fire difficult.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Electra Fire began on July 4, 2022, near Electra Road and Hwy 49, southeast of Jackson in Amador County. The fire burned 4,478 acres and caused evacuations in the area. One injury was reported. The cause is under investigation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of monsoon thunderstorms impacted the mountains and High Desert in Southern California. The San Bernardino County Mountains were particularly hard hit. Torrential downpours led to flash flooding and debris flows, including over the El Dorado burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding across the High Desert. Two to three feet of water across the roadway at SR-18 and Johnson Road north of Phelan. SR-247 at Rabbit Springs closed due to flooding. Also, report from MPing of street/road flooding with road closures. Vehicles stranded by flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. No changes were made to drought severity levels for the month of July. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with a portion of the Sierra Nevada in Tuolumne and Calaveras counties in exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and less water will mean communities across California are going to continue to see broad reaching impacts this year during the drought. Sierra Pacific Industries, owner of more than 1.7 million acres of timberland in California, closed its California forestlands to the public on July 1 due to extreme drought conditions and increasing risk of wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of July, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept all of Zone 15 in extreme drought (D3). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year at around 50-60% of average. Drought impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, increased fire danger, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy continues to be significantly impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second much stronger and colder early winter storm system brought more heavy snowfall to the Sierra late Sunday night through midweek.  A much weaker low-pressure system preceded this main event winter storm Saturday through Sunday.  A couple of waves of moderate to heavy snowfall followed and brought travel impacts to region passes through Tuesday. Lower-elevation snow showers then moved into the region Monday morning and became more widespread through Tuesday. Snow showers continued through Wednesday as the central low ejected further east across the Great Basin bringing only minor accumulations to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter, located 2.0 mi SSW of Crowley Lake, CA, and a COCORAHS observer (CA-MN-6), located 5.0 mi NNW of Lee Vining, CA, reported 24 hour snowfall amounts of 16.0 inches with 1.85 inches of liquid equivalent, and 16.9 inches, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat occurred across the Coachella Valley and the San Diego County Deserts on July 21st and 22nd with temperatures exceeding 110 degrees in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat occurred in the San Diego County Dserts on July 21st, when Ocotillo Wells recorded a high temperature of 117 degrees and Borrego Springs recorded a high of 109. Similar temperatures occurred on the 22nd, when Ocotillo Wells recorded a high of 117 degrees and Borrego Springs topped out at 111 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure centered near the four corners area built westward into California and strengthened over the area providing for a period of dangerous heat over the San Joaquin Valley, lower Sierra foothills and the Kern River Valley during the afternoon hours.  Daytime highs generally ranged between 103 and 108 degrees each day except on July 17 when daytime generally ranged between 107 and 112 degrees.  The high weakened on July 21 and 22, but maximum temperatures remained in the triple digits at most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 103 and 108 degrees on July 16 and July 18-20 and between 107 and 112 degrees on July 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. No changes were made to drought severity levels for the month of July. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with a portion of the Sierra Nevada in Tuolumne and Calaveras counties in exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and less water will mean communities across California are going to continue to see broad reaching impacts this year during the drought. Sierra Pacific Industries, owner of more than 1.7 million acres of timberland in California, closed its California forestlands to the public on July 1 due to extreme drought conditions and increasing risk of wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of July, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept the majority of Zone 19 in extreme drought (D3) with the far western border of the zone remaining in severe drought (D2). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year at around 60-85% of average. Drought impacts include but are not limited too: agriculture, water supplies, recreation, and ecosystems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low pressure system dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska to off the Pacific Northwest coast on November 6. This system remained near the Pacific Northwest coast on November 7 and 8 before moving inland across Oregon during the morning of November 9 and into the intermountain west region later that day. A large trough associated with this system pushed into central California on November 7 and spread widespread precipitation into the area which continued until the morning of November 9. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELs above 6000 feet picking up between 20 and 30 inches of new snowfall. The Sierra foothills picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of rainfall while most of the San Joaquin Valley received a half inch to an inch of rain while the much of Kern County Deserts had a quarter to half an inch of rainfall. The system also brought strong southerly winds most noticeably to the higher elevations of Kern County on November 8. Several stations in the mountains and deserts area of Kern County reported wind gusts exceeding 50 mph during the late morning and early afternoon of November 8.  A few stations above Tejon Pass measured gusts exceeding 75 mph. Scattered thunderstorms developed over the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and November 8 with a few of them moving into the Sierra foothills.  Some of the thunderstorm produced locally heavy rainfall and strong outflow winds in addition to quarter to half inch diameter hail. Several thousand customers in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills lost power during the afternoon of November 8 either due to the strong winds, wet roadways causing accidents or thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Poison Ridge SNOTEL (6988 feet) picked up an estimated 21 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Rices Fire began when weather conditions were dry with relative humidity values around 13% and temperatures in the lower 90s. Southwest winds generally 5 to 8 mph with gusts up to 15 mph were observed with nearby ridge top gusts up to 20 mph. The fire experienced a moderate rate of spread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rices Fire began on June 28, 2022, near Rices Crossing Road and Cranston Road, south of Dobbins in Nevada County. The fire burned 904 acres and caused evacuations and power outages through the area, with 1 structure destroyed. No injuries reported. The fire was not fully contained until 2:22 pm, July 11, 2022.
