EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough moving across the region created a strong pressure gradient across southeast California. Strong northerly wind gusts of up to 60 mph as well as dense blowing dust that dropped visibilities to less than 1/4 of a mile were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph gust measured at the Imperial County Airport (KIPL).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated fire weather conditions may have aided in the spread of a nine acre wildfire that burned two houses, a shop storage, and some farm equipment. Relative humidity levels at the time of the fire were in the 15-20% range and northerly winds were gusting up to 30-35 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nine acre wildfire burned two houses, a shop storage, and some farm equipment. The fire first started at a house on Foster Road and then spread south due to gusty winds at the time, damaging the other structures. Three people were displaced, but there were no injuries reported. Damages estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough dragged a cold front across the area, resulting in widespread westerly winds of 40-55 mph across southeast California. The strong winds also resulted in areas of blowing dust, reducing visibilities to less than a quarter of a mile at times. The strong winds and blowing dust resulted in several road closures, creating a significant disruption to transportation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and blowing dust resulted in a semi-truck rolling over near the intersection of Gentry Road and Walker Road, north of Westmorland in the Imperial Valley. The truck driver suffered moderate injury, which included a laceration to the head.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure centered over the Great Basin on July 11 and July 12  provided for a period of dangerous heat across the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills, Sierra foothills and the Kern River Valley during the afternoon hours. Daytime high temperatures generally ranged from 101 to 106 Degrees on both days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 101 and 106 degrees on July 11 and July 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure centered near the four corners area built westward into California and strengthened over the area providing for a period of dangerous heat over the San Joaquin Valley, lower Sierra foothills and the Kern River Valley during the afternoon hours.  Daytime highs generally ranged between 103 and 108 degrees each day except on July 17 when daytime generally ranged between 107 and 112 degrees.  The high weakened on July 21 and 22, but maximum temperatures remained in the triple digits at most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 103 and 108 degrees on July 16 and July 18-20 and between 107 and 112 degrees on July 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central California remained abnormally dry in July 2022. Scattered showers and thunderstorms did impact the area between the 26th and the 31st. Otherwise dry weather prevailed across the area. By the end of the month, nearly all of our area remained in an extreme or exceptional drought as most of our area has received less than a third of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The San Joaquin River Canyon was classified as being in an exceptional drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The western periphery of high-pressure centered over the Four-Corners region contributed to a surge of mid-level moisture into the Sierra and western NV. This pattern increased thunderstorm chances south of US-50 with the primary hazards being gusty/erratic outflow boundary winds to 50 mph, patchy dense blowing dust, lightning, and brief heavy rainfall that caused some flash flooding in Mono and Mineral Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US-6 is closed in both directions approximately 10 miles north of Chalfant valley due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. No changes were made to drought severity levels for the month of July. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with a portion of the Sierra Nevada in Tuolumne and Calaveras counties in exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and less water will mean communities across California are going to continue to see broad reaching impacts this year during the drought. Sierra Pacific Industries, owner of more than 1.7 million acres of timberland in California, closed its California forestlands to the public on July 1 due to extreme drought conditions and increasing risk of wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of July, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept the the northern and western portions of Zone 66 in extreme drought (D3) with the remainder of the zone in severe drought (D2). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year at around 60-80% of average. Drought impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy continues to be impacted.  Sierra Pacific Industries closed public recreation excess to its forestlands on July 1 due to extreme drought conditions and increasing risk of wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased gusty winds across the Sierra Crest and tightening thermal/pressure gradients preceded a strong winter storm system that was approaching the western US.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH03) Mammoth Mountain located 1.7 mi S of the Mammoth Mountain Ski Summit, CA reported a peak wind gust of 74 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low pressure system dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska to off the Pacific Northwest coast on November 6. This system remained near the Pacific Northwest coast on November 7 and 8 before moving inland across Oregon during the morning of November 9 and into the intermountain west region later that day. A large trough associated with this system pushed into central California on November 7 and spread widespread precipitation into the area which continued until the morning of November 9. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELs above 6000 feet picking up between 20 and 30 inches of new snowfall. The Sierra foothills picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of rainfall while most of the San Joaquin Valley received a half inch to an inch of rain while the much of Kern County Deserts had a quarter to half an inch of rainfall. The system also brought strong southerly winds most noticeably to the higher elevations of Kern County on November 8. Several stations in the mountains and deserts area of Kern County reported wind gusts exceeding 50 mph during the late morning and early afternoon of November 8.  A few stations above Tejon Pass measured gusts exceeding 75 mph. Scattered thunderstorms developed over the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and November 8 with a few of them moving into the Sierra foothills.  Some of the thunderstorm produced locally heavy rainfall and strong outflow winds in addition to quarter to half inch diameter hail. Several thousand customers in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills lost power during the afternoon of November 8 either due to the strong winds, wet roadways causing accidents or thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station 5NW Lebec measured a peak wind gust of 71 mph.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper low pressure system dropping through the Great Basin region pushed a cold front through central California on November 1 producing widespread precipitation across the area and resulting in this being the first significant winter storm of the season.  Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation with several stations above 6000 feet picking up an estimated 3 to 5 inches of new snowfall between the morning of November 1 and the morning of November 2 with a few stations picking up 6 inches of snow.  Much of the San Joaquin Valley north of Fresno County picked up between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall, but amounts were much lower further southward with most stations only picking up a few hundredths of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yosemite National Park Rangers reported 6 inches of new snowfall at Tioga Pass (9972 feet).  This resulted in the closure of Tioga Pass for the season.  There were also several SNOTEL reports of an estimated 3 to 5 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough dragged a cold front across the area, resulting in widespread westerly winds of 40-55 mph across southeast California. The strong winds also resulted in areas of blowing dust, reducing visibilities to less than a quarter of a mile at times. The strong winds and blowing dust resulted in several road closures, creating a significant disruption to transportation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway-86, west of Salton Sea, was closed in both directions between mile posts 33.7 and 67.8 due to hazardous conditions with strong winds and low visibility. A California Highway Patrol incident report also stated that there was a multi-vehicle accident along the highway, but it is unknown if this was due to the winds and dust. A few semi-trucks also rolled over. The closure began around 1510PST and lasted for more than 5 hours. It is unknown how low visibility dropped along the highway, but a webcam on the southeast side of Salton Sea dropped down to around 1/4 mile at times. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low pressure system dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska to off the Pacific Northwest coast on November 6. This system remained near the Pacific Northwest coast on November 7 and 8 before moving inland across Oregon during the morning of November 9 and into the intermountain west region later that day. A large trough associated with this system pushed into central California on November 7 and spread widespread precipitation into the area which continued until the morning of November 9. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELs above 6000 feet picking up between 20 and 30 inches of new snowfall. The Sierra foothills picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of rainfall while most of the San Joaquin Valley received a half inch to an inch of rain while the much of Kern County Deserts had a quarter to half an inch of rainfall. The system also brought strong southerly winds most noticeably to the higher elevations of Kern County on November 8. Several stations in the mountains and deserts area of Kern County reported wind gusts exceeding 50 mph during the late morning and early afternoon of November 8.  A few stations above Tejon Pass measured gusts exceeding 75 mph. Scattered thunderstorms developed over the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and November 8 with a few of them moving into the Sierra foothills.  Some of the thunderstorm produced locally heavy rainfall and strong outflow winds in addition to quarter to half inch diameter hail. Several thousand customers in the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills lost power during the afternoon of November 8 either due to the strong winds, wet roadways causing accidents or thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Huntington Lake SNOTEL (7020 feet) picked up an estimated 22 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first significant winter snow storm of the season brought treacherous driving conditions including chain controls and multiple highway closures due to accumulating snow, multiple accidents and spin outs due to snow. This included an extended closure on I-80 due to multiple spin outs. Reports of 9 to 17 inches of new snow in the higher mountains were received, with light accumulations down to the upper foothills, around 2,800 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CalTrans reported a storm total of 16 inches of snow at Castle Peak and 15 inches at Kingvale. Central Sierra Snow Lab reported 17 inches for the entire event, while a CDEC SNOTEL reported 8 inches at Blue Canyon. Eastbound I-80 was closed on November 1 from 4 pm until 6:35 pm to all traffic and I-80 was closed to big rigs in both directions with chain controls in effect for Sierra highways. I-80 was closed again around 7 am November 2 after an accident near the summit. I-80 eastbound remained closed until 11:05 am November 2 from Cisco Grove east. US 50 was closed from 5:15 pm to 6 pm on November 1, and again on the morning of November 2. Closure info is courtesy of CalTrans District 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms across portions of southeast CA, mainly from the late morning through the late afternoon hours. Strong southerly flow out ahead of the low pressure system brought in highly anomalous moisture content across the southern half of Arizona, with PWATs surging to 1.1-1.4, which are near record values for mid-October. Along with the highly anomalous moisture values, moderate to high instability values were also present with MLCAPE rising to 1000-1500 J/KG. Bulk shear values between 25-35 kts were also present. The combination of the highly anomalous moisture content, instability, and shear along with the strong dynamic forcing to the east and north of the low pressure system was a recipe for these showers and thunderstorms to produce flash flooding as well as severe wind gusts and small hail. The area of the greatest impacts occurred across the Imperial Valley, where several reports of flooding along with damaging winds and small hail were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water running along the eastbound lanes of I-10 resulted in closure of the highway near Ford Dry Lake Road. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms across portions of southeast CA, mainly from the late morning through the late afternoon hours. Strong southerly flow out ahead of the low pressure system brought in highly anomalous moisture content across the southern half of Arizona, with PWATs surging to 1.1-1.4, which are near record values for mid-October. Along with the highly anomalous moisture values, moderate to high instability values were also present with MLCAPE rising to 1000-1500 J/KG. Bulk shear values between 25-35 kts were also present. The combination of the highly anomalous moisture content, instability, and shear along with the strong dynamic forcing to the east and north of the low pressure system was a recipe for these showers and thunderstorms to produce flash flooding as well as severe wind gusts and small hail. The area of the greatest impacts occurred across the Imperial Valley, where several reports of flooding along with damaging winds and small hail were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Car Stranded along Senator Wash Road about 5 miles south-southwest of Martinez Lake due to flooding. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours across portions of southeast California and southwest Arizona in response to upper-level energy rotating around the base of a cutoff low located over northern Sonora. Moderate to strong instability (MLCAPE of 1000-2000 J/KG) and above normal moisture with PWATs up to 1.4 inches, aided for some of the thunderstorm activity to be significant. One thunderstorm in particular developed just north of Desert Center, CA, producing rainfall accumulations in excess of 2 inches, according to doppler radar estimates, which was enough to result in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along CA 177 near the border of Joshua Tree National Park, just north of Desert Center, two washes were completely flooded. The California Highway Patrol requested for Road Flooded signs to keep cars from driving into the flooded roadway. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought heavy snow to the Sierra Nevada and southern Cascades. Reports of 15 to 44 inches of new snow were received in the northern Sierra, with heavy snow reported down to around 3000 feet in the southern Cascades. Northbound Interstate 5 was closed 10 miles north of Fawndale for approximately 5 hours on the night of the 7th and again for about 4 hours on the 8th due to winter weather conditions. During the event, chain controls were in place on Interstate 5, Interstate 80, US Highway 50 and other mountain highways. A brief EF-0 tornado was confirmed 4 miles north northwest of Galt on the morning of the 8th, with minor damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lower Lassen Peak reported 9 of new snow on the 7th, 9 on the 8th, and 16 on the 9th for a total of 34. Travel was impacted on mountain roads with chain controls and delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought snow to Siskiyou county in northern California. While amounts were not excessive, it was the first major storm of the year and it caused closure on Interstate 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 5 in south central Siskiyou county was closed for approximately 5 hours beginning at 07/2040 PST.  This was due to snow on the roadway causing trucks to jackknife and block the road.  Interstate 5 was closed again at 08/0840 PST for at least several hours.  In addition, a spotter 2 SSW Weed reported 10.0 inches of heavy wet snow overnight at 08/0751 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought heavy snows to Siskiyou County, particularly the Mt. Shasta region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 2SSW of Weed reported 11 inches of snow in 6 hours, ending at 01/0300 PST.  The event crossed the monthly boundary. An estimated 5.5 inches fell before midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central California remained abnormally dry in October 2022. By the end of the month, nearly all of our area remained in an extreme or exceptional drought as most of our area has received less than a third of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Piute and Walker Pass areas were classified as being in an exceptional drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper low pressure center dropped southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska on October 21 and moved into into the Pacific Northwest on October 22.  A strong cold front that was associated with this system pushed southward through central California during the afternoon and evening of October 22 which brought a period a strong winds to the south end of the Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains and Mojave Desert slopes in Kern County.  Several stations measured peak wind gusts exceeding 55 mph while a few low impact indicator sites recorded peak gusts exceeding 75 mph.  This system also brought a much cooler airmass to the area as temperatures across the area were well above normal during the first three weeks of the month were well above normal on most.  Temperatures finally cooled to near normal by the 22nd and to below normal levels behind the system on October 23.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Wells Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 96 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cutoff low was situated over southern Arizona, providing forced ascent. The low pressure resulted in very cold mid-level temperatures of -13C to -14C, which helped increase the instability with moderate to high levels observed (MLCAPE of 1000-2000 J/KG). High levels of moisture were also observed with PWAT ranging between 1-1.4, which is near record values for early October. The combination of all of these factors led to the development of strong to severe thunderstorms, developing initially across far southwestern Arizona during the early afternoon hours and then moving westward into Imperial County as the afternoon progressed. A well-organized outflow boundary surged well ahead of the thunderstorm complex, resulting in dense blowing dust, which traversed westward across the Imperial Valley and into the Salton Sea area. The blowing dust activity reached as far north as Joshua Tree National Park. Thunderstorms that moved into Imperial County resulted in areas of Flash Flooding along Ogilby Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 mph gust was measured at the Imperial County Airport from thunderstorm outflow winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms across portions of southeast CA, mainly from the late morning through the late afternoon hours. Strong southerly flow out ahead of the low pressure system brought in highly anomalous moisture content across the southern half of Arizona, with PWATs surging to 1.1-1.4, which are near record values for mid-October. Along with the highly anomalous moisture values, moderate to high instability values were also present with MLCAPE rising to 1000-1500 J/KG. Bulk shear values between 25-35 kts were also present. The combination of the highly anomalous moisture content, instability, and shear along with the strong dynamic forcing to the east and north of the low pressure system was a recipe for these showers and thunderstorms to produce flash flooding as well as severe wind gusts and small hail. The area of the greatest impacts occurred across the Imperial Valley, where several reports of flooding along with damaging winds and small hail were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy flooding reported on the on and off ramp of South 4th street and I-8 in El Centro. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cutoff low was situated over southern Arizona, providing forced ascent. The low pressure resulted in very cold mid-level temperatures of -13C to -14C, which helped increase the instability with moderate to high levels observed (MLCAPE of 1000-2000 J/KG). High levels of moisture were also observed with PWAT ranging between 1-1.4, which is near record values for early October. The combination of all of these factors led to the development of strong to severe thunderstorms, developing initially across far southwestern Arizona during the early afternoon hours and then moving westward into Imperial County as the afternoon progressed. A well-organized outflow boundary surged well ahead of the thunderstorm complex, resulting in dense blowing dust, which traversed westward across the Imperial Valley and into the Salton Sea area. The blowing dust activity reached as far north as Joshua Tree National Park. Thunderstorms that moved into Imperial County resulted in areas of Flash Flooding along Ogilby Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video on social media showed visibility near zero in Calexico due to blowing dust. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Santa Ana winds impacted the Inland Empire starting late on November 15th and lasting through the afternoon hours on the 16th. The strongest winds occurred during the early morning hours on the 16th. The Rancho Cucamonga SoCal-Edison mesonet site recorded a peak wind gust of 75 mph. The CSU San Bernardino SoCal-Edison mesonet site also recorded a peak wind gust of 73 mph.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper low pressure center dropped southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska on October 21 and moved into into the Pacific Northwest on October 22.  A strong cold front that was associated with this system pushed southward through central California during the afternoon and evening of October 22 which brought a period a strong winds to the south end of the Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains and Mojave Desert slopes in Kern County.  Several stations measured peak wind gusts exceeding 55 mph while a few low impact indicator sites recorded peak gusts exceeding 75 mph.  This system also brought a much cooler airmass to the area as temperatures across the area were well above normal during the first three weeks of the month were well above normal on most.  Temperatures finally cooled to near normal by the 22nd and to below normal levels behind the system on October 23.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker Pass RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought heavy snow to the Sierra Nevada and southern Cascades. Reports of 15 to 44 inches of new snow were received in the northern Sierra, with heavy snow reported down to around 3000 feet in the southern Cascades. Northbound Interstate 5 was closed 10 miles north of Fawndale for approximately 5 hours on the night of the 7th and again for about 4 hours on the 8th due to winter weather conditions. During the event, chain controls were in place on Interstate 5, Interstate 80, US Highway 50 and other mountain highways. A brief EF-0 tornado was confirmed 4 miles north northwest of Galt on the morning of the 8th, with minor damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down near Highway 99 and Arno Rd., about 4 miles NNW of Galt. The California Highway Patrol reported a tin roof from a barn blown off and downed powerlines over Arno Rd. and a bus had windows blown out. Maximum estimated wind speeds were 70 mph. Path length was approximately 0.8 miles, path width 400 yards,  |from preliminary information.

The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below-average precipitation for the water year. Drought impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, increased fire danger, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy continues to be impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. For the month of November, there were no changes made to drought severity levels in the region. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with portions of the northern San Joaquin as high as exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by Governor Gavin Newsom continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and have less water for those purposes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of November, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept the northern and western portions of Zone 66 in extreme drought (D3) with the remainder of the zone in severe drought (D2). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below-average precipitation for the water year. Drought impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy continues to be impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of October 7th, dense fog developed around Coastal Monterey Bay and in the Salinas Valley. A Dense Fog Advisory was issued for the Northern Salinas Valley, Monterey Bay and Big Sur Coast with visibilities reported at a quarter of a mile or less. The fog created travel hazards and disruptions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CalTrans reported a 7-vehicle collision involving two big rigs and four passenger cars on Rte-101 just north of Underwood, near Greenfield. Northbound lanes were blocked.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough moving across the region created a strong pressure gradient across southeast California. Strong northerly wind gusts of up to 60 mph as well as dense blowing dust that dropped visibilities to less than 1/4 of a mile were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized pockets of under 1/4 mile visibility with blowing dust reported on I-10 near Mile Marker 210, which slowed/stopped traffic in the area. Lofted dust also visible on Caltrans Camera near Desert Center looking east. No damages or injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms over Mohave County, Arizona made it across the Colorado River and produced wind damage in Needles before dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Waves estimated to be six feet high swept across Lake Havasu due to severe thunderstorm winds. Multiple boaters in distress were rescued from their crafts and from the water. One person was trapped under a capsized craft near the Havasu Landing Casino and drowned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms over Mohave County, Arizona made it across the Colorado River and produced wind damage in Needles before dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down power lines and large trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep-layer westerly flow occurring along a zonally oriented quasi-stationary frontal boundary yielded a multi-day atmospheric river event across Northwest California during late December. Widespread impacts due to heavy rain and strong winds were observed across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: East Barbara Lane Bridge at Railroad Avenue was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold and dry airmass which had settled over the San Joaquin Valley on November 19 remained in place on November 20. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 28 and 32 degrees on November 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry low pressure system dropped into the Great Basin during the morning of November 28 which resulted in an increased surface pressure gradient over central California which provided for a period of gusty winds over the ridge tops in Kern County as well as along the Mojave Desert slopes. Several stations measured gusts between 45 and 55 mph while some low impact indicator sites had gusts of 60 mph or higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker Pass RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Yeti Wildfire started off as two separate blazes, the China 2 and the Evans 2. The fires were started by lightning strikes around 11 PM PST on 07/28/22.  They combined and were renamed the Yeti fire on 08/02/22. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall report of 6 inches at an elevation above 2800 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure that was centered over the Great Basin area built westward into California on August 31st and brought a period of hot and dry weather to the area which prevailed across the area through much of early September. An Excessive Heat Warning was issued for a large portion of the area including all of the San Joaquin Valley, the West Side Hills, the Sierra Foothills, Yosemite Valley, The Kern River Valley and the Kern County desert areas and remained in effect until the evening hours of September 9.  The large high pressure area finally broke down on September 9 and temperatures remained below dangerous levels on September 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs generally ranged between 103 and 108 degrees on September 1, 3 and 4, between 105 and 110 degrees on September 2, 5, 8 and 9 and between 110 and 115 degrees on September 6 and 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central California remained abnormally dry in September 2022. A record setting heat wave on the first nine days of the month made the ongoing drought conditions worse as the intense heat continued to dry out the soil. The heat wave was followed by scattered showers and thunderstorms between the 10th and the 12th as moisture from Hurricane Kay surged into the area. A winter like storm brought some light precipitation to the area on September 18 and 19. However, these events did little to improve the long term drought conditions. By the end of the month, nearly all of our area remained in an extreme or exceptional drought as most of our area has received less than a third of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yosemite Valley was classified as being in an exceptional drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over interior northern California brought widespread high to very high heat risk to the region. Triple-digit high temperatures were observed across most of the Central Valley, Delta, and foothills through the first 9 days of September. The hottest temperatures were on September 5 and 6, when temperatures exceeding 110 were observed at many locations. All-time records were set on the 6th for Sacramento stations and tied in Stockton. Numerous daily and monthly record high-temperature were also set in the area. There was one death attributed to the heat in Sacramento County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake Shasta Dam saw high temperatures reach 100 to 111 degrees during the event. The hottest days were on the 6th when the high was 110 and 111 on the 7th; these were both daily records. Morning lows were also high, dropping only to 78 on the 8th and 80 on the 9th. The minimum of 80 was the highest ever in September, breaking the record of 79 set on the 10th in 2011.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep-layer westerly flow occurring along a zonally oriented quasi-stationary frontal boundary yielded a multi-day atmospheric river event across Northwest California during late December. Widespread impacts due to heavy rain and strong winds were observed across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: PG&E weather station measured a wind gust to 65 mph on Monument Ridge above Rio Dell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening trough off of the West Coast enhanced by an upper jet led to large-scale ascent over the Bay Area, and particularly the Central Coast, as a series of cold fronts swept through the area over a three day period from November 5th-7th. These favorable dynamics and forcings brought widespread stratiform rainfall to the Bay Area and Central Coast. Behind the last of the fronts, a cold unstable airmass settled into the region, bringing scattered convective showers and thunderstorms over land and water, thus increasing flooding and bringing gusty winds. A Flash Flood Watch was issued for the Colorado and River Burn Scars in Monterey County for November 8th, along with Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories for large swaths of Santa Cruz and Santa Clara Counties, and portions of Monterey, San Benito and Alameda Counties. A Wind Advisory was also issued for coastal areas effective November 8th. Storm total rainfall was generally 0.75 to 2 but was higher in the Santa Cruz Mountains (2-3+) and in the Santa Lucia's (2-5+).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unconscious surfer was rescued off China Beach in turbulent waters. A high surf advisory was in effect at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold and dry airmass which had settled over the San Joaquin Valley on November 19 remained in place on November 20. With skies remaining mainly clear and winds remaining light, another night of strong radiational cooling took place as a result; and temperatures dropped below the freezing mark for three or more hours across most of the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of November 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 28 and 32 degrees on November 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold airmass settled into central California on November 29 following the passage of a dry cold front the previous day. With clear skies and light winds prevailing strong radiational cooling took place across the San Joaquin valley and as a result many locations dropped below the freezing mark for a few hours on November 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 29 and 32 degrees on November 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The McKinney Wildfire started around 1415 PDT on 07/29/22.  The cause remains under investigation.  Initially, it spread slowly, but winds from nearby thunderstorms caused it to spread rapidly on 07/30/22. As of the last report at 0530 PST on 09/07/22, the fire covered 60138 acres and was 100% contained. 185 structures were lost, and 75.8 million had been spent on firefighting efforts. Four people were killed by the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure that was centered over the Great Basin area built westward into California on August 31st and brought a period of hot and dry weather to the area which prevailed across the area through much of early September. An Excessive Heat Warning was issued for a large portion of the area including all of the San Joaquin Valley, the West Side Hills, the Sierra Foothills, Yosemite Valley, The Kern River Valley and the Kern County desert areas and remained in effect until the evening hours of September 9.  The large high pressure area finally broke down on September 9 and temperatures remained below dangerous levels on September 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs generally ranged between 103 and 108 degrees on September 1, 3 and 4, between 105 and 110 degrees on September 2, 5, 8 and 9 and between 110 and 115 degrees on September 6 and 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central California remained abnormally dry in September 2022. A record setting heat wave on the first nine days of the month made the ongoing drought conditions worse as the intense heat continued to dry out the soil. The heat wave was followed by scattered showers and thunderstorms between the 10th and the 12th as moisture from Hurricane Kay surged into the area. A winter like storm brought some light precipitation to the area on September 18 and 19. However, these events did little to improve the long term drought conditions. By the end of the month, nearly all of our area remained in an extreme or exceptional drought as most of our area has received less than a third of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sequoia National Park was classified as being in an exceptional drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over interior northern California brought widespread high to very high heat risk to the region. Triple-digit high temperatures were observed across most of the Central Valley, Delta, and foothills through the first 9 days of September. The hottest temperatures were on September 5 and 6, when temperatures exceeding 110 were observed at many locations. All-time records were set on the 6th for Sacramento stations and tied in Stockton. Numerous daily and monthly record high-temperature were also set in the area. There was one death attributed to the heat in Sacramento County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern Sacramento Valley region saw all-time, monthly and daily record highs set, reaching up to 116 degrees during the event. On the 6th Downtown Sacramento recorded a high temperature of 116, which broke the all-time record high of 114 degrees set on July 17, 1925. This also broke the September record of 113 which was set the day before on the 5th. The previous monthly record of 109 was set on September 1, 1950, and tied on September 2, 1955 and September 6, 2020. The record of consecutive days over 110 was tied at 2. Daily highs were also set with 113 on the 5th and 8th and 109 on the 7th. The low of 80 on the 7th was the warmest on record in September and in all months was surpassed only on July 23, 2006, which had a low of 84. Sacramento International Airport set an all-time daily record high of 116 on the 5th and 6th, and also set a record of 4 consecutive days of 110 or higher. Sacramento Executive Airport set an all-time daily record high of 114 on the 5th and 6th. The Sacramento County Coroner attributed the death of one unhoused 49 year old man to the heat, having died while in a hospital.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Delta regions saw high temperatures reach up to 116 degrees. Fairfield/Travis AFB reached 116 on the 5th and 6th, while the COOP station in Fairfield recorded 114 on each date. Highs remained above 100 from the 1st to the 10th. The highest low was 75 on the 7th at Fairfield COOP, and 76 at Fairfield/Travis AFB on the 6th.

The fog was most noticeable in Madera where traffic along SR 99 was impacted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Madera.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. For the month of September, there were no changes made to drought severity levels. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with portions of the northern San Joaquin as high as exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by Governor Gavin Newsom continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and have less water for those purposes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of September, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept all of Zone 13 in extreme drought (D3). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year. Drought impacts include but are not limited too: agriculture, water supplies, recreation, and ecosystems.

Several stations measured gusts between 45 and 55 mph while a few low impact indicator sites had gusts of 60 mph or higher.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison Station at Little Oak Canyon measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper low pressure system dropping through the Great Basin region pushed a cold front through central California on November 1 producing widespread precipitation across the area and resulting in this being the first significant winter storm of the season.  Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation with several stations above 6000 feet picking up an estimated 3 to 5 inches of new snowfall between the morning of November 1 and the morning of November 2 with a few stations picking up 6 inches of snow.  Much of the San Joaquin Valley north of Fresno County picked up between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall, but amounts were much lower further southward with most stations only picking up a few hundredths of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Devils Post Pile SNOTEL (8004 feet) picked up an estimated 6 inches of new snow.  There were also several SNOTEL reports of an estimated 3 to 5 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High clouds moving over the area during the morning of November 11 inhibited dense fog from forming in the San Joaquin Valley, but the clouds moved out of the area overnight and skies had cleared out by the morning of November 12. With light winds and inversion condition present, a narrow strip of dense fog formed by daybreak along SR 99 between Tulare and Selma where visibility was reduced to a few hundred feet and traffic was impacted along SR 99 for a few hours until the fog burned off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Hanford. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although a significant winter storm impacted central California between November 7 and November 9, the interior remained abnormally dry in November 2022 as most of the area had slightly below normal precipitation totals for the month. By the end of the month, nearly all of our area remained in an extreme or exceptional drought as most of our area has received less than a third of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bakersfield area was classified as being in an exceptional drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure that was centered over the Great Basin area built westward into California on August 31st and brought a period of hot and dry weather to the area which prevailed across the area through much of early September. An Excessive Heat Warning was issued for a large portion of the area including all of the San Joaquin Valley, the West Side Hills, the Sierra Foothills, Yosemite Valley, The Kern River Valley and the Kern County desert areas and remained in effect until the evening hours of September 9.  The large high pressure area finally broke down on September 9 and temperatures remained below dangerous levels on September 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs generally ranged between 100 and 105 degrees on September 1, 3 and 4, between 102 and 107 degrees on September 2, 5, 8 and 9 and between 107 and 112 degrees on September 6 and 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central California remained abnormally dry in September 2022. A record setting heat wave on the first nine days of the month made the ongoing drought conditions worse as the intense heat continued to dry out the soil. The heat wave was followed by scattered showers and thunderstorms between the 10th and the 12th as moisture from Hurricane Kay surged into the area. A winter like storm brought some light precipitation to the area on September 18 and 19. However, these events did little to improve the long term drought conditions. By the end of the month, nearly all of our area remained in an extreme or exceptional drought as most of our area has received less than a third of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Frazier Mountain Communities were classified as being in an exceptional drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. For the month of September, there were no changes made to drought severity levels. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with portions of the northern San Joaquin as high as exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by Governor Gavin Newsom continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and have less water for those purposes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of September, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept all of Zone 15 in extreme drought (D3). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year. Drought impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, increased fire danger, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy continues to be significantly impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over interior northern California brought widespread high to very high heat risk to the region. Triple-digit high temperatures were observed across most of the Central Valley, Delta, and foothills through the first 9 days of September. The hottest temperatures were on September 5 and 6, when temperatures exceeding 110 were observed at many locations. All-time records were set on the 6th for Sacramento stations and tied in Stockton. Numerous daily and monthly record high-temperature were also set in the area. There was one death attributed to the heat in Sacramento County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northeastern foothills of the Sacramento Valley saw high temperatures reach upwards of 106 to 112 degrees during the heat event, with very warm lows in the upper 70s to 90. The hottest days were the 5th and 6th. Cohasset had a high of 110 with a low of 86 on the 5th, a high of 112, and a low of 90 on the 6th.  Jarbo Gap had a high of 106 and a low of 77 on the 5th and a high of 109 and a low of 81 on the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With skies clearing out and winds diminishing during the morning of November 4 across the Mojave Desert, temperatures dropped below the freezing mark resulting in the first freeze of the season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 25 and 30 degrees on November 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold and dry airmass which had settled over the San Joaquin Valley on November 19 continued to hold it's grip over the area on November 21. Although some high clouds moved over the area during the day, skies cleared out overnight and with winds remaining light, another night of strong radiational cooling took place. Once again temperatures dropped below the freezing mark for three or more hours across most of the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of November 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 28 and 32 degrees on November 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mill Fire started in the town of Weed at 1249 PDT on 09/02/22. The cause was under investigation.  The fire spread rapidly in the town.  As of the last report at 0530 PST on 9/13/22, the fire covered 3935 acres and was 100 percent contained.  118 structures were lost and 14.5 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.  Two people were killed by the fire.

The previous monthly record high was 93 set on September 5, 2020, and the previous monthly record maximum low was 77 set on September 6, 2020. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening trough off of the West Coast enhanced by an upper jet led to large-scale ascent over the Bay Area, and particularly the Central Coast, as a series of cold fronts swept through the area over a three day period from November 5th-7th. These favorable dynamics and forcings brought widespread stratiform rainfall to the Bay Area and Central Coast. Behind the last of the fronts, a cold unstable airmass settled into the region, bringing scattered convective showers and thunderstorms over land and water, thus increasing flooding and bringing gusty winds. A Flash Flood Watch was issued for the Colorado and River Burn Scars in Monterey County for November 8th, along with Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories for large swaths of Santa Cruz and Santa Clara Counties, and portions of Monterey, San Benito and Alameda Counties. A Wind Advisory was also issued for coastal areas effective November 8th. Storm total rainfall was generally 0.75 to 2 but was higher in the Santa Cruz Mountains (2-3+) and in the Santa Lucia's (2-5+).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person drowned and another was rescued in the turbulent waters off Pacifica's Linda Mar Beach. No beach hazard product was in effect at the time. CDIP buoy off Point Reyes showed significant swell heights of around 7-8 feet at 12 seconds, and wind waves of around 6 feet at 9 seconds, at time of incident.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fresno and Tulare County Lower Sierra were classified as being in an exceptional drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mountain Wildfire started at 1801 PDT on 09/02/22. The cause was under investigation. As of the last report at 0530 PST on 09/18/22, the fire covered 13440 acres and was 100 percent contained.  4 structures were lost and 46.0 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mountain Wildfire started at 1801 PDT on 09/02/22. The cause was under investigation. As of the last report at 0530 PST on 09/18/22, the fire covered 13440 acres and was 100 percent contained.  4 structures were lost and 46.0 million dollars had been spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from Tropical Storm Kay which was centered just off the Baja coast surged northward into central California during the morning of September 10. While the deeper moisture remained to the south and east of central California, enough moisture made it into the area and combined with an unstable airmass left over the area in the wake of a heat wave that persisted for several days over the region. Widespread showers and scattered thunderstorms fired up across the area during the afternoon of September 10 and moved rapidly toward the northwest. The rapid movement of the thunderstorms inhibited flooding potential, but several locations in the higher Sierra Nevada and the Tehachapi Mountains picked up between a quarter to a half inch of rainfall. Amounts in the San Joaquin Valley and the Kern County Desert areas generally ranged from a trace to a few hundredths of an inch of rainfall with the most noticeable impacts being from outflow winds from some of the stronger cells over the Sierra Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Incident Meteorologist at the nearby Red Fire in Yosemite Park reported a car damaged by a large tree branch which was knocked down by strong thunderstorm outflow gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While the remnants of Tropical Storm Kay took a westward turn off of the northern Baja coast and away from our area, enough moisture from the surge that moved over central California during the previous day remained in place; and with daytime heating resulting in widespread instability over the area; another round of showers and thunderstorms broke out during the afternoon of September 11. While rainfall amounts were generally light, one slow moving thunderstorm produced heavy rainfall in the Sierra Nevada over the 2021 KNP Fire burn scar which resulted in a mudslide over Generals Highway in Sequoia National Park and closed the highway for several hours until the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide occurred on the Generals Highway between Quail Flat and the intersection  with SR 180. This resulted in a lengthy road closure as Generals Highway remained closed until the following morning for clean-up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from Tropical Storm Kay which surged northward into central California on September 10 remained over our area as steering currents over the region became weak. This allowed for scattered slow moving showers and thunderstorms to develop over the area during the afternoon of September 12. While rainfall amounts were once again generally light, several stations the Sierra Nevada, Tehachapi Mountains and the desert areas in Kern County picked up between a tenth and a third of an inch of rainfall. A few thunderstorms did produce locally heavier rainfall amounts and resulted in roadway flooding in the Tehachapi area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported 8 vehicles were stuck behind roadway flooding at the intersection of Sand Canyon Rd. and Pine Canyon Rd. east of Tehachapi.

CZ_NAME: MOUNTAINS SOUTHWESTERN SHASTA COUNTY TO WESTERN COLUSA COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. For the month of September, there were no changes made to drought severity levels. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with portions of the northern San Joaquin as high as exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by Governor Gavin Newsom continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and have less water for those purposes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of September, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept the majority of Zone 63 in extreme drought (D3) with the far western border of the zone remaining in severe drought (D2). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year. Drought impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, increased fire danger, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy continues to be impacted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of September, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept the southern portion of Zone 69 in Calaveras and Tuolumne counties in exceptional drought (D4), and kept a large region in the southwestern portion of the zone in extreme drought (D3) and the remainder of the zone in severe drought (D2). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year. Drought impacts include but are not limited too: agriculture, water supplies, recreation, and ecosystems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over interior northern California brought widespread high to very high heat risk to the region. Triple-digit high temperatures were observed across most of the Central Valley, Delta, and foothills through the first 9 days of September. The hottest temperatures were on September 5 and 6, when temperatures exceeding 110 were observed at many locations. All-time records were set on the 6th for Sacramento stations and tied in Stockton. Numerous daily and monthly record high-temperature were also set in the area. There was one death attributed to the heat in Sacramento County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Northern Sacramento Valley saw daily records broken, with high temperatures reaching 110 to 115 degrees, and with low temperatures reaching up to 90. Redding Airport reached 115 degrees on the 6th (old record 111 in 2020), with Red Bluff peaking at 114 (old record 113 in 2020). On the 8th Redding Airport reached 112 degrees (old record 109 in 1923), with Red Bluff peaking at 113 (old record 112 in 1944). On the 9th Redding Airport reached 112 degrees (old record 110 in 1944), with Red Bluff peaking at 112 (tying the old record of 112 in 1944). On the 9th Redding recorded a daily record high minimum temperature of 87 degrees and Red Bluff 82 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold and dry airmass which had settled over the San Joaquin Valley on November 19 continued to hold it's grip over the area on November 21. Although some high clouds moved over the area during the day, skies cleared out overnight and with winds remaining light, another night of strong radiational cooling took place. Once again temperatures dropped below the freezing mark for three or more hours across most of the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of November 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 28 and 32 degrees on November 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold and dry airmass which had settled over the San Joaquin Valley on November 19 moderated slightly, but provided the area with another night of sub-freezing temperatures across portions of the San Joaquin valley as skies were mainly clear and winds remained light allowing for temperatures to drop below the freezing mark across the center portion of the San Joaquin Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 29 and 32 degrees on November 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep-layer westerly flow occurring along a zonally oriented quasi-stationary frontal boundary yielded a multi-day atmospheric river event across Northwest California during late December. Widespread impacts due to heavy rain and strong winds were observed across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused mud, dirt, and rock to slide across HWY 36 between Carlotta and Bridgeville. The road was closed throughout slide removal.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough pushed through the region on September 19 and 20.  This system brought scattered showers to the area mainly impacting areas from Fresno County northward.  The snow level remained above 10000 feet for most of the event, but lowered to 8000 feet during the morning of September 20; and much of Yosemite Park above 8000 feet picked up between 1 and 3 inches of new snowfall between the morning of September 20 and the early morning of September 21 during this early season event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 1 and 3 inches of new snow fell above 8000 feet across much of Yosemite National Park on September 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter storm brought strong winds and mountain snow to the area. Peak wind gusts in the mountains of Los Angeles county reached 97 MPH while gusts up to 80 MPH were reported across the Antelope Valley. In the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties, winter storm conditions were observed with the strong winds and snowfall accumulations up to around 12 inches above 7000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty southwesterly winds impacted the Antelope Valley. Some peak wind reports from the local mesonet include: Cruthers Creek (gust 80 MPH), Juniper Mesa (gust 69 MPH), Fort Tejon Road (gust 67 MPH), Palmdale Airport (gust 64 MPH) and Lake Palmdale RAWS (gust 60 MPH).

In the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties, winter storm conditions were observed with the strong winds and snowfall accumulations up to around 12 inches above 7000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty southwesterly winds impacted the mountains of San Luis Obispo county. Wind gusts of 58 MPH were reported at Mount Lowe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early September heat starting in August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at San Diego International Airport observed daily highs reaching at least the middle to upper 80s through much of early September, with several days reaching the lower to middle 90s. The highest daily maximum temperature of 95 degrees occurred on the afternoon of Saturday September 3rd, with a high of 94 occurring on Thursday, September 8th. Highs on Saturday, September 10th were significantly cooler due to clouds and rain associated with the approach of Hurricane Kay, which ended the heat wave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early September heat starting in August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at John Wayne International Airport observed daily highs reaching at least the lower to middle 90s through much of early September, with several days ranging from 97 to 106 degrees. The highest daily maximum temperature of 106 degrees occurred on the afternoon of Sunday September 4th, with a high of 102 occurring on Thursday, September 8th. Highs on Saturday, September 10th were significantly cooler due to clouds and rain associated with the approach of Hurricane Kay, which ended the heat wave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early September heat starting in August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Ramona Airport observed daily highs ranging from 100 to 107 degrees through much of early September. The highest daily maximum temperature of 107 degrees occurred on the afternoon of Wednesday September 7th, with a high of 104 degrees occurring on all of the September 1st, 4th, and 6th. Highs on Saturday, September 10th were significantly cooler due to clouds and rain associated with the approach of Hurricane Kay, which ended the heat wave.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central California remained abnormally dry in September 2022. A record setting heat wave on the first nine days of the month made the ongoing drought conditions worse as the intense heat continued to dry out the soil. The heat wave was followed by scattered showers and thunderstorms between the 10th and the 12th as moisture from Hurricane Kay surged into the area. A winter like storm brought some light precipitation to the area on September 18 and 19. However, these events did little to improve the long term drought conditions. By the end of the month, nearly all of our area remained in an extreme or exceptional drought as most of our area has received less than a third of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Side Mountains north of Highway 198 were classified as being in an exceptional drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second much stronger and colder early winter storm system brought more heavy snowfall to the Sierra late Sunday night through midweek.  A much weaker low-pressure system preceded this main event winter storm Saturday through Sunday.  A couple of waves of moderate to heavy snowfall followed and brought travel impacts to region passes through Tuesday. Lower-elevation snow showers then moved into the region Monday morning and became more widespread through Tuesday. Snow showers continued through Wednesday as the central low ejected further east across the Great Basin bringing only minor accumulations to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the summit of the Mammoth Mountain Ski Base reported 24 hour snow amounts of 15 inches, and a storm total snow of 20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second much stronger and colder early winter storm system brought more heavy snowfall to the Sierra late Sunday night through midweek.  A much weaker low-pressure system preceded this main event winter storm Saturday through Sunday.  A couple of waves of moderate to heavy snowfall followed and brought travel impacts to region passes through Tuesday. Lower-elevation snow showers then moved into the region Monday morning and became more widespread through Tuesday. Snow showers continued through Wednesday as the central low ejected further east across the Great Basin bringing only minor accumulations to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public located at the summit of Homewood Mountain Ski resort and Palisades Tahoe Ski Base both reported storm total snow amounts of 8.0 inches,  whereas, a trained spotter from the Central Sierra Snow Lab (CSSL), located 6.0 mi WSW of Truckee, CA  reported 17.9 inches of new snowfall and 1.44 inches of liquid equivalent in the last 24 hours. Storm total snowfall for this storm was reported as 34.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry low pressure system dropped into the Great Basin during the morning of November 28 which resulted in an increased surface pressure gradient over central California which provided for a period of gusty winds over the ridge tops in Kern County as well as along the Mojave Desert slopes. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Fairview Fire began burning around 3:37 PM PDT on September 5th at the intersection of Fairview Avenue and Bautista Road in Hemet. Evacuation orders were quickly put in place and then expanded several hours later. The fire consumed 28,307 acres, destroyed 35 properties, and damaged 6 properties before full containment. Two fatalities were also attributed to the Fairview Fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fairview Fire began burning around 3:37 PM PDT on September 5th at the intersection of Fairview Avenue and Bautista Road in Hemet. Evacuation orders were quickly put in place and then expanded several hours later. The fire consumed 28,307 acres, destroyed 35 properties, and damaged 6 properties before full containment. Two fatalities were also attributed to the Fairview Fire. As of 7:38 AM PDT on September 17th, the fire was 98% contained with crews working to patrol and for and monitor any smoldering material.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture and instability from Hurricane Kay generated strong thunderstorms and heavy rain across sections of Southern California. Across the deserts, mountains and foothills, rainfall totals between 1 and 2.30 inches were observed. Strong thunderstorms generated several flash flooding events across the area. The most significant flash flooding event was near Lake Hughes in Los Angeles county where 53 people had to be extracted from their vehicles which were stuck in mud and debris.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flash flooding along Highway 166, west of New Cuyama. The flooding was the result of heavy rain from strong thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure that was centered over the Great Basin area built westward into California on August 31st and brought a period of hot and dry weather to the area which prevailed across the area through much of early September. An Excessive Heat Warning was issued for a large portion of the area including all of the San Joaquin Valley, the West Side Hills, the Sierra Foothills, Yosemite Valley, The Kern River Valley and the Kern County desert areas and remained in effect until the evening hours of September 9.  The large high pressure area finally broke down on September 9 and temperatures remained below dangerous levels on September 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs generally ranged between 103 and 108 degrees on September 1, 3 and 4, between 105 and 110 degrees on September 2, 5, 8 and 9 and between 110 and 115 degrees on September 6 and 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid to upper-level low pressure system moved southward and parked itself off the north-central California coast. Widespread rainfall occurred on the 18th, with some embedded heavier rain. The next couple of days showers and isolated thunderstorms |observed with locally very heavy rain. One such shower brought just over 2.5 inches of rain in 45 minutes near the town of Willits and caused some flooding on the Willits Bypass. Several SPSs and FLAs were issued during the span of a couple days for stronger thunderstorms and the accompanying heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS report of 2.58 inches of rain in 45 minutes. 1 to 2 inches of water was observed on the Willits Bypass which stopped traffic. Water quickly subsided after the thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early September heat starting in August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Beaumont AWOS site observed daily highs ranging from the upper 90s to upwards of 105 degrees through much of early September. The highest daily maximum temperature of 105 degrees occurred on both the afternoons of Thursday, September 1st and Tuesday September 6th, with a high of 102 degrees occurring on both the 4th and 5th of September. Highs on Friday, September 9th were significantly cooler due to clouds and rain associated with the approach of Hurricane Kay, which ended the heat wave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Dutch Fire started on September 13th at 1 pm PDT on I-80WB, near the Dutch Flat off-ramp. The cause is under investigation. The fire started in very hot and dry weather in an area with drought conditions with exceptionally dry vegetation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dutch Fire started on September 13th at 1 pm PDT on I-80WB, near the Dutch Flat off-ramp. Winds were generally light over the area, around 5-10 mph with gusts less than 20 mph. Humidity hovered in the 20-30% range with temperatures in the upper 70s.|Around 1:30 pm PDT lane and ramp closures of I-80 began, along with evacuations of nearby residents. At 2 pm PDT I-80WB was closed and by 3:15 PM PDT it was closed in both directions. At 3:10 pm PDT a power outage was reported along I-80 from Applegate to Colfax, impacting 1785 customers. Around 5:30 pm PDT reports of progress stopped and evacuations were lifted, with 26 acres burned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mosquito Fire began in Placer County 4 miles east of Foresthill near Mosquito Ridge Road, CA, and close to Oxbow Reservoir the evening of September 6, 2022, at 6:27 PM PDT and later spread into El Dorado County, CA. The cause is under investigation. The fire started in extreme heat and very low humidity, in an area with drought conditions with exceptionally dry vegetation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mosquito Fire started on September 6th, which had all-time record high temperatures in the area set and extremely low humidity dropping into the single digits. Extreme fire behavior was observed due to the very dry humidity and fuels, with the fire developing large plumes that radar indicated extended up to 40,000 feet. In the first few days, the fire saw rapid growth at 5,705 acres by 7 pm PDT on the 7th and 13,705 acres by 8 pm PDT on the 8th. More than 11,000 people were evacuated and 9,000 structures were threatened. The fire included areas in both the Tahoe and Eldorado National Forests. The fire burned a total of 76,788 acres and caused road closures throughout the area. There were 2 firefighters injured during the fire. A total of 78 structures were destroyed and an additional 13 buildings were damaged in the towns of Foresthill, Volcanoville, and Michigan Bar. Periods of moderate to heavy rain from September 18-21 largely halted fire growth, but the fire was not considered fully contained until October 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak push of monsoon moisture plus ongoing record heat fueled a few thunderstorms which produced flash flooding in Death Valley National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rocks were washed onto Hwy 190 at Towne Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak push of monsoon moisture plus ongoing record heat fueled a few thunderstorms which produced flash flooding in Death Valley National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water, mud, debris, and rocks closed Hwy 190 between mile markers 110 and 115 with about 20 vehicles unable to get through. The entry road to The Inn was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter storm brought strong winds and mountain snow to the area. Peak wind gusts in the mountains of Los Angeles county reached 97 MPH while gusts up to 80 MPH were reported across the Antelope Valley. In the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties, winter storm conditions were observed with the strong winds and snowfall accumulations up to around 12 inches above 7000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter storm conditions developed across the mountains of Los Angeles county. Heavy snowfall was reported around the resort level with snowfall totals in the 6-18 inch range. Along with the snow, very strong and gusty southwest winds were reported. Some peak gusts from the local mesonet include: Magic Mountain Truck Trail (gust 97 MPH), Sierra Peloma (gust 90 MPH), Ford Observatory (gust 91 MPH), Mill Creek RAWS (gust 70 MPH), Camp Nine RAWS (gust 66 MPH) and Chilao (gust 66 MPH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season winter storm brought strong winds and mountain snow to the area. In the mountains of Ventura and Los Angeles counties, winter storm conditions were observed with the strong winds and snowfall accumulations up to around 12 inches above 7000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter storm conditions developed across the mountains of Ventura county. Heavy snowfall was reported at and above the 6000 foot level with snowfall totals in the 6-12 inch range. Along with the snow, very strong and gusty southwest winds were reported. Peak gusts between 60 and 70 MPH were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong and gusty Santa Ana winds impacted many areas of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Peak wind gusts in excess of 100 MPH were reported in the mountains with gusts between 60 and 80 MPH across other sections of Ventura and Los Angeles counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty Santa Ana winds impacted the Southeastern Valleys of Ventura county. Some peak northeasterly wind gusts from the local mesonet include: Simi Valley at Cochran Road (gust 72 MPH) and Miller Ranch (gust 59 MPH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought gusty winds and increased chances for mountain snow and valley showers. Temperatures cooled well seasonal average with some of the coldest air to date spreading into the region behind the exiting cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure that was centered over the Great Basin area built westward into California on August 31st and brought a period of hot and dry weather to the area which prevailed across the area through much of early September. An Excessive Heat Warning was issued for a large portion of the area including all of the San Joaquin Valley, the West Side Hills, the Sierra Foothills, Yosemite Valley, The Kern River Valley and the Kern County desert areas and remained in effect until the evening hours of September 9.  The large high pressure area finally broke down on September 9 and temperatures remained below dangerous levels on September 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs generally ranged between 103 and 108 degrees on September 1, 3 and 4, between 105 and 110 degrees on September 2, 5, 8 and 9 and between 110 and 115 degrees on September 6 and 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central California remained abnormally dry in September 2022. A record setting heat wave on the first nine days of the month made the ongoing drought conditions worse as the intense heat continued to dry out the soil. The heat wave was followed by scattered showers and thunderstorms between the 10th and the 12th as moisture from Hurricane Kay surged into the area. A winter like storm brought some light precipitation to the area on September 18 and 19. However, these events did little to improve the long term drought conditions. By the end of the month, nearly all of our area remained in an extreme or exceptional drought as most of our area has received less than a third of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mariposa and Madera County Lower Sierra were classified as being in an exceptional drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pacific trough draws two atmospheric rivers, November 8-9, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ontario Fire department reported 6 person in the swiftwater channel in John Galvin park where the channel begins near I-10. Rainfall rates of 0.50 to 0.75 inches per hour had occurred from 7 am to 9 am in Ontario and Rancho Cucamonga. 3 persons were rescued. 3 more person were later found drowned in a retaining pond which was filled 15 to 20 feet high with storm runoff water. The bodies were not found until several days later due the water being too deep and low visibility. Multiple agencies were involved in the week long recovery efforts. The channel runs south past Ontario airport to where it reached the storage collection ponds on East Philadelphia Street. Search and rescue in Cucamonga Channel reported by CalOES and Ontario Fire Department. As many as 10 people experiencing homelessness were swept away as a surge of water occurred following 2 hours of heavy rain. Five people were immediately rescued, one person immediately reported deceased, with two additional bodies recovered (one of November 14, the other on November 16).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system dropped southeast out of the northeast Pacific into central California during the afternoon of December 1 which resulted in widespread precipitation across the area between the morning of December 1 until the morning of December 2. Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of beneficial liquid precipitation while elevations above 6000 feet generally picked up between 1 to 2 feet of new snowfall. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall with slightly higher amounts being picked up in Merced County while Kern County had noticeably less precipitation with most stations picking up between a tenth and two tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph while a few low impact indicator sites in Kern County measured gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Green Mountain SNOTEL (7900 feet) picked up an estimated 21 inches of new snowfall.
