EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system slowly moved across central California on December 10 and 11 providing the area with moderate to heavy precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet with several SNOTEL stations picking up between 18 and 30 inches of estimated new snowfall. The snow level lowered to near 3000 feet by the afternoon of December 11 with some stations in the Sierra Nevada and higher foothills picking up between 3 and 6 inches of new snowfall. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada received between 3 and 5 inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of rainfall and much of the San Joaquin Valley and Tehachapi Mountains received between half an inch and an inch of rainfall. The precipitation did change over the snow in the Tehachapi Mountains above 4000 feet, but snowfall amounts were generally light. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation across the Kern County Deserts with most stations receiving between a tenth of an inch and a quarter inch of rainfall. The precipitation tapered off during the evening of December 11 and ended by midday on December 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a debris flow with boulders on the roadway on SR 140 5NNW Midpines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. For the month of August, the only changes made to drought severity levels were to the northern San Joaquin Valley and southern portion of the Motherlode. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with the southern portions of the region as high as  exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and have less water for those purposes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of August, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept all of Zone 16 in extreme drought (D3). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year. Drought impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, increased fire danger, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy continues to be significantly impacted.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system slowly moved across central California on December 10 and 11 providing the area with moderate to heavy precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet with several SNOTEL stations picking up between 18 and 30 inches of estimated new snowfall. The snow level lowered to near 3000 feet by the afternoon of December 11 with some stations in the Sierra Nevada and higher foothills picking up between 3 and 6 inches of new snowfall. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada received between 3 and 5 inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of rainfall and much of the San Joaquin Valley and Tehachapi Mountains received between half an inch and an inch of rainfall. The precipitation did change over the snow in the Tehachapi Mountains above 4000 feet, but snowfall amounts were generally light. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation across the Kern County Deserts with most stations receiving between a tenth of an inch and a quarter inch of rainfall. The precipitation tapered off during the evening of December 11 and ended by midday on December 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tenaya Lake SNOTEL (8150 feet) picked up an estimated 21 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds along with strong radiational cooling and a low level inversion made for favorable conditions for areas of dense fog to form by daybreak. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet in Madera and Merced Counties where several school districts opened late for the first time this season due to dense fog. Patches of dense fog were also prevalent during the morning hours further south in Selma and Hanford. The fog completely dissipated by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Atwater, Merced and Madera. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet. Several school districts opened late because of dense fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low cloud deck remained over the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and evening of Christmas Day. The low cloud deck lowered back down to the valley floor overnight and widespread dense fog prevailed for the fourth consecutive morning over the San Joaquin Valley on December 26. By late morning the flog lifted into a stratus deck which finally dissipated by late afternoon as an incoming storm provided for some mixing to take place over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Bakersfield. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low pressure system moved through California on December 27. This system had abundant moisture associated with it as it pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin. This system brought widespread moderate to heavy precipitation to the area between the early morning until the early evening of December 27. The heavy rainfall produced a rockslide in Yosemite National Park which resulted in two fatalities. The precipitation tapered off by the early morning of December 28. Much of the higher Sierra Nevada picked up between an inch and a half and three inches of liquid precipitation with the precipitation mainly falling as snow above 7500 feet where many stations picked up between 18 and 30 inches of new snowfall. The snow level briefly lowered to around 6000 feet during the evening of December 27 and some light snowfall was observed between 6000 and 7500 feet. The Sierra foothills generally received between 1 to 2 inches of rain while 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley and the Tehachapi Mountains. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported by law enforcement and on social media. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the Kern County Deserts to a quarter inch or less. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the mountains as several stations measured gusts exceeding 40 mph and a few low impact stations had brief gusts above 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Yosemite Pilot Peak measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. For the month of August, the only changes made to drought severity levels were to the northern San Joaquin Valley and southern portion of the Motherlode. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with the southern portions of the region as high as  exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and have less water for those purposes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of August, the U.S. Drought Monitor degraded interior portions of Zone 19 to exceptional drought (D4) and kept the remainder of the zone in extreme drought (D3) with the far western border of the zone in severe drought (D2). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year. Drought impacts include but are not limited too: agriculture, water supplies, recreation, and ecosystems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite the abundant rainfall and replenishing higher elevation snowpack received across the area in December, a long term precipitation deficit has resulted in continuing drought conditions across the central California Interior. Extreme drought conditions continued across most of the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts. Drought conditions did improve across the Sierra Nevada to moderate to severe conditions due to the abundant rainfall in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Visalia, Porterville and Reedley areas were classified as being in an extreme drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough moved slowly through California on December 30 and 31. This system had a deep fetch of tropical moisture associated with it which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy precipitation along with widespread flooding across much of the area on December 31. Numerous reports of flooding and debris flows were reported by law enforcement with several roads being briefly closed as a result of flooding. The system also produced strong wind gusts across the area between the afternoon of December 30 and the morning of January 1 with several stations in the West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada below 7500 feet picked up between 4 and 7 inches of rainfall while the adjacent foothills picked up 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the San Joaquin Valley to between half an inch and an inch of rain at most locations while many stations in the Tehachapi Mountains also picked up between half an inch and an inch of rain. Several higher elevations SNOTEL stations picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall adding to the heavy snowpack which had already accumulated earlier in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Joaquin Ridge measured a peak wind gust of 83 mph.

Several higher elevations SNOTEL stations picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall adding to the heavy snowpack which had already accumulated earlier in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Pelato Peak measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

CZ_NAME: MOUNTAINS SOUTHWESTERN SHASTA COUNTY TO WESTERN COLUSA COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. For the month of August, the only changes made to drought severity levels were to the northern San Joaquin Valley and southern portion of the Motherlode. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with the southern portions of the region as high as  exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and have less water for those purposes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of August, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept the majority of Zone 63 in extreme drought (D3) with the far western border of the zone remaining in severe drought (D2). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year. Drought impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, increased fire danger, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy continues to be impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared out across portions of the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of December 2. This resulted in some localized dense fog across the area with visibility as low as eighth of a mile being briefly reported in Merced and in Hanford. Visibility rapidly improved by late morning as the fog dissipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Merced and Atwater.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough which moved through the region on December 3 and 4 brought another round of widespread precipitation to the area. This system picked up some warm tropical moisture which resulted in snow levels being rather high for much of the duration of this event. While significant snowfall did fall above 8000 feet, moderate to locally heavy rainfall below the snow line as much of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 2 and 4 inches of rainfall while the adjacent foothills picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of rain and the San Joaquin Valley picked up between half an inch and an inch of rainfall. A rockslide was reported near Mariposa which resulted in some brief road closures. Much like with the December 1 and 2 storm, amounts were much lower in Kern County where most stations only picked up a tenth of an inch or less of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several SNOTEL reports of an estimated 5 to 10 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low pressure system moved through California on December 27. This system had abundant moisture associated with it as it pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin. This system brought widespread moderate to heavy precipitation to the area between the early morning until the early evening of December 27. The heavy rainfall produced a rockslide in Yosemite National Park which resulted in two fatalities. The precipitation tapered off by the early morning of December 28. Much of the higher Sierra Nevada picked up between an inch and a half and three inches of liquid precipitation with the precipitation mainly falling as snow above 7500 feet where many stations picked up between 18 and 30 inches of new snowfall. The snow level briefly lowered to around 6000 feet during the evening of December 27 and some light snowfall was observed between 6000 and 7500 feet. The Sierra foothills generally received between 1 to 2 inches of rain while 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley and the Tehachapi Mountains. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported by law enforcement and on social media. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the Kern County Deserts to a quarter inch or less. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the mountains as several stations measured gusts exceeding 40 mph and a few low impact stations had brief gusts above 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crabtree Meadow SNOTEL (10700 feet) picked up an estimated 23 inches of new snowfall.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite the abundant rainfall and replenishing higher elevation snowpack received across the area in December, a long term precipitation deficit has resulted in continuing drought conditions across the central California Interior. Extreme drought conditions continued across most of the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts. Drought conditions did improve across the Sierra Nevada to moderate to severe conditions due to the abundant rainfall in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Side Mountains north of Highway 198 were classified as being in an extreme drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

Several higher elevations SNOTEL stations picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall adding to the heavy snowpack which had already accumulated earlier in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported flooding on SR 152 at the Merced College entrance west of Los Banos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved eastward across central California during the afternoon and evening of December 6. As the low moved east of the area during the morning of December 7, skies cleared out and winds diminished across portions of the San Joaquin Valley and Tehachapi Mountains allowing for areas of dense fog to form as there was plenty of low level moisture around as a result of recent rainfall across the area. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Delano. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of high pressure which built in over central California during the day on December 7 kept skies mainly clear and winds light overnight into the morning of December 8. This provided for favorable conditions for areas of dense fog to form towards daybreak in the San Joaquin Valley most noticeably in Merced County and near Hanford.  The fog burned off by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Atwater and Madera.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger storm system impacted Southern California on New Year's Eve with heavier precipitation and higher winds. Multiple reports of flooding and high winds were received on New Year's Eve alone. Heavy precipitation and wind continued into January 1st with impacts detailed in a different episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of flooding across all lanes on SR-22 East at Main Street. Rainfall totals generally between 0.70 and 1.20 inch occurred across this area on New Year's Eve, with localized higher totals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system slowly moved across central California on December 10 and 11 providing the area with moderate to heavy precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet with several SNOTEL stations picking up between 18 and 30 inches of estimated new snowfall. The snow level lowered to near 3000 feet by the afternoon of December 11 with some stations in the Sierra Nevada and higher foothills picking up between 3 and 6 inches of new snowfall. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada received between 3 and 5 inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of rainfall and much of the San Joaquin Valley and Tehachapi Mountains received between half an inch and an inch of rainfall. The precipitation did change over the snow in the Tehachapi Mountains above 4000 feet, but snowfall amounts were generally light. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation across the Kern County Deserts with most stations receiving between a tenth of an inch and a quarter inch of rainfall. The precipitation tapered off during the evening of December 11 and ended by midday on December 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding on SR J59 in Snelling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system dropped southeast out of the northeast Pacific into central California during the afternoon of December 1 which resulted in widespread precipitation across the area between the morning of December 1 until the morning of December 2. Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of beneficial liquid precipitation while elevations above 6000 feet generally picked up between 1 to 2 feet of new snowfall. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall with slightly higher amounts being picked up in Merced County while Kern County had noticeably less precipitation with most stations picking up between a tenth and two tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph while a few low impact indicator sites in Kern County measured gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tuolumne Meadows SNOTEL (8600 feet) picked up an estimated 22 inches of new snowfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred across the San Diego County Mountains on December 31st. A peak wind gust of 60 mph occurred at an observation site on Cuyamaca Peak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. For the month of August, the only changes made to drought severity levels were to the northern San Joaquin Valley and southern portion of the Motherlode. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with the southern portions of the region as high as  exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and have less water for those purposes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of August, the U.S. Drought Monitor expanded the southern portion of Zone 67 in Calaveras and Tuolumne counties in exceptional drought (D4), and severe drought (D2) in the far northeastern portion of the zone as well as the remainder of the zone in extreme drought (D3). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year. Drought impacts include but are not limited too: agriculture, water supplies, recreation, and ecosystems.

CZ_NAME: YOSEMITE NP OUTSIDE OF THE VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system dropped southeast out of the northeast Pacific into central California during the afternoon of December 1 which resulted in widespread precipitation across the area between the morning of December 1 until the morning of December 2. Much of the Sierra Nevada picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of beneficial liquid precipitation while elevations above 6000 feet generally picked up between 1 to 2 feet of new snowfall. Much of the San Joaquin Valley picked up between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall with slightly higher amounts being picked up in Merced County while Kern County had noticeably less precipitation with most stations picking up between a tenth and two tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph while a few low impact indicator sites in Kern County measured gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Paradise Meadow SNOTEL (7676 feet) picked up an estimated 24 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system slowly moved across central California on December 10 and 11 providing the area with moderate to heavy precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet with several SNOTEL stations picking up between 18 and 30 inches of estimated new snowfall. The snow level lowered to near 3000 feet by the afternoon of December 11 with some stations in the Sierra Nevada and higher foothills picking up between 3 and 6 inches of new snowfall. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada received between 3 and 5 inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of rainfall and much of the San Joaquin Valley and Tehachapi Mountains received between half an inch and an inch of rainfall. The precipitation did change over the snow in the Tehachapi Mountains above 4000 feet, but snowfall amounts were generally light. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation across the Kern County Deserts with most stations receiving between a tenth of an inch and a quarter inch of rainfall. The precipitation tapered off during the evening of December 11 and ended by midday on December 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding at the intersection of Westside and Lincoln Boulevards in Livingston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. For the month of August, the only changes made to drought severity levels were to the northern San Joaquin Valley and southern portion of the Motherlode. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with the southern portions of the region as high as  exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and have less water for those purposes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of August, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept all of Zone 15 in extreme drought (D3). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year. Drought impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, increased fire danger, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy continues to be significantly impacted.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE OF FRESNO AND KINGS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds prevailed across central California during the evening of December 16 which allowed for widespread dense fog to develop across the San Joaquin Valley during the morning of December 17. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck across most of the valley by late afternoon, but persisted in some locations until the morning of December 18. A stratus deck persisted across most of the valley until the afternoon of December 19 when mixing provided by a storm to the north of our area helped dissipate the low cloud deck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Lemoore. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared out across much of the San Joaquin Valley during the late afternoon of December 23 which allowed for widespread dense fog to form during the evening as light winds and inversion conditions prevailed under high pressure. The fog persisted across much of the valley until the early afternoon of December 24 when it lifted into a low cloud deck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Fresno. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low pressure system moved through California on December 27. This system had abundant moisture associated with it as it pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin. This system brought widespread moderate to heavy precipitation to the area between the early morning until the early evening of December 27. The heavy rainfall produced a rockslide in Yosemite National Park which resulted in two fatalities. The precipitation tapered off by the early morning of December 28. Much of the higher Sierra Nevada picked up between an inch and a half and three inches of liquid precipitation with the precipitation mainly falling as snow above 7500 feet where many stations picked up between 18 and 30 inches of new snowfall. The snow level briefly lowered to around 6000 feet during the evening of December 27 and some light snowfall was observed between 6000 and 7500 feet. The Sierra foothills generally received between 1 to 2 inches of rain while 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley and the Tehachapi Mountains. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported by law enforcement and on social media. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the Kern County Deserts to a quarter inch or less. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the mountains as several stations measured gusts exceeding 40 mph and a few low impact stations had brief gusts above 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bear Peak RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low pressure system moved through California on December 27. This system had abundant moisture associated with it as it pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin. This system brought widespread moderate to heavy precipitation to the area between the early morning until the early evening of December 27. The heavy rainfall produced a rockslide in Yosemite National Park which resulted in two fatalities. The precipitation tapered off by the early morning of December 28. Much of the higher Sierra Nevada picked up between an inch and a half and three inches of liquid precipitation with the precipitation mainly falling as snow above 7500 feet where many stations picked up between 18 and 30 inches of new snowfall. The snow level briefly lowered to around 6000 feet during the evening of December 27 and some light snowfall was observed between 6000 and 7500 feet. The Sierra foothills generally received between 1 to 2 inches of rain while 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley and the Tehachapi Mountains. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported by law enforcement and on social media. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the Kern County Deserts to a quarter inch or less. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the mountains as several stations measured gusts exceeding 40 mph and a few low impact stations had brief gusts above 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported rocks on the road blocking east bound lanes of Huntington Lake Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite the abundant rainfall and replenishing higher elevation snowpack received across the area in December, a long term precipitation deficit has resulted in continuing drought conditions across the central California Interior. Extreme drought conditions continued across most of the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts. Drought conditions did improve across the Sierra Nevada to moderate to severe conditions due to the abundant rainfall in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South End Sierra Foothills were classified as being in an extreme drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS SOUTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough moved slowly through California on December 30 and 31. This system had a deep fetch of tropical moisture associated with it which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy precipitation along with widespread flooding across much of the area on December 31. Numerous reports of flooding and debris flows were reported by law enforcement with several roads being briefly closed as a result of flooding. The system also produced strong wind gusts across the area between the afternoon of December 30 and the morning of January 1 with several stations in the West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada below 7500 feet picked up between 4 and 7 inches of rainfall while the adjacent foothills picked up 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the San Joaquin Valley to between half an inch and an inch of rain at most locations while many stations in the Tehachapi Mountains also picked up between half an inch and an inch of rain. Several higher elevations SNOTEL stations picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall adding to the heavy snowpack which had already accumulated earlier in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Twisselman RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. For the month of August, the only changes made to drought severity levels were to the northern San Joaquin Valley and southern portion of the Motherlode. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with the southern portions of the region as high as  exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and have less water for those purposes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of August, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept the the northern and western portions of Zone 66 in extreme drought (D3) with the remainder of the zone in severe drought (D2). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year. Drought impacts to the region include; little to no allocation of water from irrigation districts to agriculture, reduced rangeland forage without alternative forage, hay prices at record levels, cattle being sold, including calves that are typically sold in late Fall, as well as increasing reports of dry wells. The local economy continues to be impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system slowly moved across central California on December 10 and 11 providing the area with moderate to heavy precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet with several SNOTEL stations picking up between 18 and 30 inches of estimated new snowfall. The snow level lowered to near 3000 feet by the afternoon of December 11 with some stations in the Sierra Nevada and higher foothills picking up between 3 and 6 inches of new snowfall. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada received between 3 and 5 inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of rainfall and much of the San Joaquin Valley and Tehachapi Mountains received between half an inch and an inch of rainfall. The precipitation did change over the snow in the Tehachapi Mountains above 4000 feet, but snowfall amounts were generally light. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation across the Kern County Deserts with most stations receiving between a tenth of an inch and a quarter inch of rainfall. The precipitation tapered off during the evening of December 11 and ended by midday on December 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Graveyard Meadows SNOTEL (6897 feet) picked up an estimated 19 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and dry airmass settled in over central California in the wake of a departed winter storm on December 12 and 13. With skies clearing out and winds diminishing, temperatures dropped below the freezing mark for a few hours across the rural areas of the San Joaquin Valley during the mornings of December 14, 15, 16 and 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 29 and 32 degrees on December 14, 15, 16 and 17.

This system had abundant moisture associated with it as it pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin. This system brought widespread moderate to heavy precipitation to the area between the early morning until the early evening of December 27. The heavy rainfall produced a rockslide in Yosemite National Park which resulted in two fatalities. The precipitation tapered off by the early morning of December 28. Much of the higher Sierra Nevada picked up between an inch and a half and three inches of liquid precipitation with the precipitation mainly falling as snow above 7500 feet where many stations picked up between 18 and 30 inches of new snowfall. The snow level briefly lowered to around 6000 feet during the evening of December 27 and some light snowfall was observed between 6000 and 7500 feet. The Sierra foothills generally received between 1 to 2 inches of rain while 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley and the Tehachapi Mountains. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported by law enforcement and on social media. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the Kern County Deserts to a quarter inch or less. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the mountains as several stations measured gusts exceeding 40 mph and a few low impact stations had brief gusts above 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported both sides of the roadway flooded at S. Reed Ave. and E. Annadale Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite the abundant rainfall and replenishing higher elevation snowpack received across the area in December, a long term precipitation deficit has resulted in continuing drought conditions across the central California Interior. Extreme drought conditions continued across most of the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts. Drought conditions did improve across the Sierra Nevada to moderate to severe conditions due to the abundant rainfall in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coalinga and Avenal areas were classified as being in an extreme drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough moved slowly through California on December 30 and 31. This system had a deep fetch of tropical moisture associated with it which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy precipitation along with widespread flooding across much of the area on December 31. Numerous reports of flooding and debris flows were reported by law enforcement with several roads being briefly closed as a result of flooding. The system also produced strong wind gusts across the area between the afternoon of December 30 and the morning of January 1 with several stations in the West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada below 7500 feet picked up between 4 and 7 inches of rainfall while the adjacent foothills picked up 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the San Joaquin Valley to between half an inch and an inch of rain at most locations while many stations in the Tehachapi Mountains also picked up between half an inch and an inch of rain. Several higher elevations SNOTEL stations picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall adding to the heavy snowpack which had already accumulated earlier in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walker Pass RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter located 6 miles NW of McCloud reports 21 inches of snow ending at 0800 on 12/10.  Also,  spotter located 2 SSW of Weed at 3900 feet elevation reports 18 inches of snow ending at 0817 on 12/10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold Pacific Storm System Produces Urban Flooding and Heavy Mountain Snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High-elevation snow impacted the Riverside County Mountains from the morning on December 11 through December 12. Snow accumulations of 2 to 4 inches were reported above 5000 feet. Light snow was reported as low as 2500 feet near Beaumont, but did not stick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. For the month of August, the only changes made to drought severity levels were to the northern San Joaquin Valley and southern portion of the Motherlode. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with the southern portions of the region as high as  exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries continue to be significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and have less water for those purposes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of August, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept the majority of Zone 17 in extreme drought (D3). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year. Some of the impacts the region saw included; reduced forage, decreased stock weights, livestock stress, selling of livestock, decrease in water allocation, increased reports of dry wells including domestic wells, hauling of water, and fallowed areas of cropland.

The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year. Some of the impacts the region saw included; reduced forage, decreased stock weights, livestock stress, selling of livestock, decrease in water allocation, increased reports of dry wells including domestic wells, hauling of water, and fallowed areas of cropland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system slowly moved across central California on December 10 and 11 providing the area with moderate to heavy precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet with several SNOTEL stations picking up between 18 and 30 inches of estimated new snowfall. The snow level lowered to near 3000 feet by the afternoon of December 11 with some stations in the Sierra Nevada and higher foothills picking up between 3 and 6 inches of new snowfall. Many stations in the Sierra Nevada received between 3 and 5 inches of liquid precipitation while the adjacent foothills picked up between 1.5 and 3 inches of rainfall and much of the San Joaquin Valley and Tehachapi Mountains received between half an inch and an inch of rainfall. The precipitation did change over the snow in the Tehachapi Mountains above 4000 feet, but snowfall amounts were generally light. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation across the Kern County Deserts with most stations receiving between a tenth of an inch and a quarter inch of rainfall. The precipitation tapered off during the evening of December 11 and ended by midday on December 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported roadway flooding on Interstate 5 just north of the intersection with SR 33 north of Coalinga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and dry airmass settled in over central California in the wake of a departed winter storm on December 12 and 13. With skies clearing out and winds diminishing, temperatures dropped below the freezing mark for a few hours across the rural areas of the San Joaquin Valley during the mornings of December 14, 15, 16 and 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several locations reported minimum temperatures between 29 and 32 degrees on December 14, 15, 16 and 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Skies cleared out across the central portion of the San Joaquin Valley following the passage of an upper level low pressure system which had moved through northern California. The clearing skies and light winds made conditions favorable for denes fog to form over the central portion of the San Joaquin Valley. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Delano. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent low cloud deck inhibited fog formation across most of the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of December 20 through the morning of December 21. However, skies had cleared out during the afternoon of December 20 form Madera northward and over portions of the west side of the valley allowing for fog to form overnight. The fog lifted into a low cloud deck by late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Atwater, Merced and Madera. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low cloud deck remained over the San Joaquin Valley during the afternoon and evening of Christmas Day. The low cloud deck lowered back down to the valley floor overnight and widespread dense fog prevailed for the fourth consecutive morning over the San Joaquin Valley on December 26. By late morning the flog lifted into a stratus deck which finally dissipated by late afternoon as an incoming storm provided for some mixing to take place over the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility below a quarter mile was reported in Fresno. Several webcams showed visibility below 1000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low pressure system moved through California on December 27. This system had abundant moisture associated with it as it pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin. This system brought widespread moderate to heavy precipitation to the area between the early morning until the early evening of December 27. The heavy rainfall produced a rockslide in Yosemite National Park which resulted in two fatalities. The precipitation tapered off by the early morning of December 28. Much of the higher Sierra Nevada picked up between an inch and a half and three inches of liquid precipitation with the precipitation mainly falling as snow above 7500 feet where many stations picked up between 18 and 30 inches of new snowfall. The snow level briefly lowered to around 6000 feet during the evening of December 27 and some light snowfall was observed between 6000 and 7500 feet. The Sierra foothills generally received between 1 to 2 inches of rain while 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rain fell in the San Joaquin Valley and the Tehachapi Mountains. Several reports of nuisance flooding were reported by law enforcement and on social media. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the Kern County Deserts to a quarter inch or less. This system also brought some strong wind gusts to the mountains as several stations measured gusts exceeding 40 mph and a few low impact stations had brief gusts above 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported SR 140 entrance to Yosemite closed due to a rockfall 3 miles east of the tollbooths.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS SOUTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite the abundant rainfall and replenishing higher elevation snowpack received across the area in December, a long term precipitation deficit has resulted in continuing drought conditions across the central California Interior. Extreme drought conditions continued across most of the San Joaquin Valley and Kern County Deserts. Drought conditions did improve across the Sierra Nevada to moderate to severe conditions due to the abundant rainfall in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Side Mountains south of Highway 198 were classified as being in an extreme drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper trough moved slowly through California on December 30 and 31. This system had a deep fetch of tropical moisture associated with it which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy precipitation along with widespread flooding across much of the area on December 31. Numerous reports of flooding and debris flows were reported by law enforcement with several roads being briefly closed as a result of flooding. The system also produced strong wind gusts across the area between the afternoon of December 30 and the morning of January 1 with several stations in the West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations in the Sierra Nevada below 7500 feet picked up between 4 and 7 inches of rainfall while the adjacent foothills picked up 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. Rain shadowing effects limited precipitation in the San Joaquin Valley to between half an inch and an inch of rain at most locations while many stations in the Tehachapi Mountains also picked up between half an inch and an inch of rain. Several higher elevations SNOTEL stations picked up 18 to 30 inches of new snowfall adding to the heavy snowpack which had already accumulated earlier in December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Wells Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold Pacific Storm System Produces Urban Flooding and Heavy Mountain Snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High-elevation snow impacted the San Bernardino County Mountains from the morning on December 11 through December 12. Snow accumulations of 2 to 8 inches were common at elevations above 4500 feet, with some notable amounts:|10 inches of snow near Running Springs|12-15 inches of snow at Snow Valley.|The Department of Highways reported three vehicles stuck in snow at 271 Fairway Drive, with as many as 80 vehicles stuck in snow on Highway 18, resulting in the closure of Highway 18 at Upper Waterman Canyon at 4000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold Pacific Storm System Produces Urban Flooding and Heavy Mountain Snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds impacted the San Bernardino County Mountains on December 11, with the peak winds occurring from midnight through the mid-morning hours. Widespread wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were observed, with local wind gusts over 80 mph reported. The following are notable wind gust reports:|Mesonet station 424SCE Silverwood Lake reported a peak wind gust of 84 mph.|Mesonet station SCE338 Cajon Lookout reported a peak wind gust of 77 mph.|Mesonet station SCE269 Cushenbury Springs reported a peak wind gust of 74 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold Pacific Storm System Produces Urban Flooding and Heavy Mountain Snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Victorville Airport reported 1/4 mile - 1 mile visibility in blowing dust for about two hours. The peak wind gust of 50 kt was reported at 2:55 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold Pacific Storm System Produces Urban Flooding and Heavy Mountain Snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail of pea size (0.25 inch) to 0.5 inch in diameter, with hail covering the road on Coronado. Accidents reported on Highway-75 between Nab Coronado and Leyte Road on the Silver Strand (Beach Highway). One vehicle flipped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold Pacific Storm System Produces Urban Flooding and Heavy Mountain Snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Palm Springs Police Department closed North Indian Canyon Drive through the Whitewater wash due to water over the road, with the closure reported at 4:26 PM and December 11, remaining closed through 1 PM on December 12. The Riverside County Mountains reported 1-2 inches of rain prior to the closure, and Palm Springs reported 0.23 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold Pacific Storm System Produces Urban Flooding and Heavy Mountain Snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat occurred across the lower deserts from July 10-12. The Harbison Wildfire was sparked in eastern San Diego County July 12-13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Kay formed on September 4th, 2022 and made its closest approach to Southern California on the 9th and 10th, with the center of Kay coming within 120 miles of San Diego. Tropical moisture associated with Kay managed to produce heavy rainfall leading to flooding across mountainous terrain in Southern California on September 9th and 10th. This led to a number of wind damage and flooding events in the mountains and desert foothill locations in Southern California. Kay transitioned to a Post-Tropical Cyclone that weekend, but left enough lingering moisture to support additional rounds of heavy thunderstorms through the 11th and 12th of September. These later rounds of heavy rain would lead to multiple significant debris flow events in the San Bernardino County Mountains, namely in and around the Apple and El Dorado burn scars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm in Murrieta produced 0.64 inches of rainfall.

CZ_NAME: MOUNTAINS OF SAN BENITO COUNTY AND INTERIOR MONTEREY COUNTY INCLUDING PINNACLES NATIONAL PARK
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure encompassed the Western CONUS from September 1st through 8th, leading to anomalously hot temperatures along the California coast, and even more extreme temperatures in interior valleys and all mountain zones, where very hot daytime temperatures combined with a lack of overnight cooling created extreme heat risk . Excessive Heat Warnings were issued for interior areas and mountains of Monterey/San Benito Counties for the period September 1st through 8th, and for interior valleys and mountain zones around the San Francisco Bay Area from September 4th through 8th. A combination of Heat Advisories and an Excessive Heat Warning was issued for parts of Monterey Bay and its near coastal valleys, the San Francisco Bay Shoreline, and Marin Coastal Mountains from September 4th through 8th, along with a Heat Advisory for the Central Coast, San Francisco, and coastal North Bay on September 6th. Several daily record high temperature records were shattered, along with a handful of monthly and all-time records. Triple digit temperatures were reported across a vast swath of the interior, with some sites reaching 110 degrees and higher. Reports of power outages, heat related illnesses and deaths were received. Counties opened/operated one or more cooling centers to provide relief from the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pinnacles NP reported a max temperature of 111 degrees. Several other unofficial sites in the surrounding mountains reported high temperatures between 102 and 108 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Kay formed on September 4th, 2022 and made its closest approach to Southern California on the 9th and 10th, with the center of Kay coming within 120 miles of San Diego. Tropical moisture associated with Kay managed to produce heavy rainfall leading to flooding across mountainous terrain in Southern California on September 9th and 10th. This led to a number of wind damage and flooding events in the mountains and desert foothill locations in Southern California. Kay transitioned to a Post-Tropical Cyclone that weekend, but left enough lingering moisture to support additional rounds of heavy thunderstorms through the 11th and 12th of September. These later rounds of heavy rain would lead to multiple significant debris flow events in the San Bernardino County Mountains, namely in and around the Apple and El Dorado burn scars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy thunderstorms associated with remnant moisture from post-tropical cyclone Kay produced torrential rain over the Apple and El Dorado burn scars on the afternoon of September 12. Multiple debris flows occurred following torrential one-hour rainfall rates exceeding debris flow guidance from the USGS and CAGS. Two of the debris flows traveled several miles including the Banning Canyon wash showing debris logs and ash water. The most impactful debris flow occurred in Forest Falls and Oak Glen, where the flow traveled at least 3 miles down Oakland Road with 4 foot boulders, logs over-topping the road with several feet of mud, with several power poles destroyed. Another highly impactful debris flow formed in Forest Falls on Prospect Road, where large boulders, black water and logs took out trees and damaged several homes. Another debris flow occurred 0.5 mile away on Valley of the Falls Road, producing a 12-foot wall of dirt and rocks. An additional debris flow occurred on State Highway Route 38 just before Angeles Oaks. These flows are the most significant that have occurred since the fires in 2020. An estimated 30 homes were damaged and three vehicles were moved and damaged.||The following are recorded rain amounts from the same region:|Raywood Flats, 6 NNW Forest Falls: 2.44 inch in 1 hour and 2.75 inch for the day, resulting in the full closure of Highway 38.||Mesonet Station Yucc1 Little San Gorgonio Peak Yucaipa Ridge, 2 SE Forest Falls: 1.06 inch in 60 minutes.||Mesonet Station FFPC1, 1.5 W Forest Falls: 1.93 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought heavy rain, mountain snow and gusty winds to Southwestern California. Rainfall totals were very impressive, ranging from 1-4 inches across coastal and valley areas to 4-8 inches across the foothills and mountains. Fortunately, only nuisance urban flooding was reported. Snow levels remained quite high during the bulk of the storm with areas above 6000 feet receiving up to 5 inches of new snowfall. Along with the precipitation, strong southwesterly wind impacted many areas with peak gust in excess of 80 MPH reported across the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty southerly winds impacted the Santa Lucia Mountains in San Luis Obispo county. Some peak wind gusts from the local mesonet include: TV Towers (gust 66 MPH), Old Creek Road (gust 66 MPH), High School Hill (gust 61 MPH) and Green Valley Road (gust 59 MPH).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Every road in Death Valley National Park was closed, and several were damaged by flooding as well. One vehicle was stuck in mud near Stovepipe Wells, and the Cow Creek water system was broken.

CZ_NAME: SANTA CLARA VALLEY INCLUDING SAN JOSE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure encompassed the Western CONUS from September 1st through 8th, leading to anomalously hot temperatures along the California coast, and even more extreme temperatures in interior valleys and all mountain zones, where very hot daytime temperatures combined with a lack of overnight cooling created extreme heat risk . Excessive Heat Warnings were issued for interior areas and mountains of Monterey/San Benito Counties for the period September 1st through 8th, and for interior valleys and mountain zones around the San Francisco Bay Area from September 4th through 8th. A combination of Heat Advisories and an Excessive Heat Warning was issued for parts of Monterey Bay and its near coastal valleys, the San Francisco Bay Shoreline, and Marin Coastal Mountains from September 4th through 8th, along with a Heat Advisory for the Central Coast, San Francisco, and coastal North Bay on September 6th. Several daily record high temperature records were shattered, along with a handful of monthly and all-time records. Triple digit temperatures were reported across a vast swath of the interior, with some sites reaching 110 degrees and higher. Reports of power outages, heat related illnesses and deaths were received. Counties opened/operated one or more cooling centers to provide relief from the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A daily record high temperature of 107 deg was set at the Gilroy Coop site, breaking the previous record 103 deg set in 1998. Various other stations reported temperatures in the upper 90s to mid 100s throughout the valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure developed over the area along with light offshore flow, allowing temperatures to soar into the 90s to low 100s for all areas, except parts of the immediate coastline. A head advisory was issued for all but coastal zones from late morning through the evening of June 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Concord and Livermore airports reached a high of 102 degrees. Other cities in the zone reached the upper 90s to low 100s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Cyclone Kay formed on September 4th, 2022 and made its closest approach to Southern California on the 9th and 10th, with the center of Kay coming within 120 miles of San Diego. Tropical moisture associated with Kay managed to produce heavy rainfall leading to flooding across mountainous terrain in Southern California on September 9th and 10th. This led to a number of wind damage and flooding events in the mountains and desert foothill locations in Southern California. Kay transitioned to a Post-Tropical Cyclone that weekend, but left enough lingering moisture to support additional rounds of heavy thunderstorms through the 11th and 12th of September. These later rounds of heavy rain would lead to multiple significant debris flow events in the San Bernardino County Mountains, namely in and around the Apple and El Dorado burn scars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station ANEC1 Alpine RAWS site measured 0.64 inch of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of monsoonal moisture coupled with a pre-storm environment of steepened late afternoon lapse rates and modest shear from an upper short wave lifting north-northeast over the region produced discrete thunderstorms capable of heavy downpours and large hail over Plumas and Lassen counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained weather spotter reported half dollar sized hail and very heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Peter Fire began when weather conditions were dry with relative humidity values around 7-14% and temperatures in the upper 90s. South to southeast winds generally 7 to 12 mph with gusts up to 15 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Peter Fire began on July 14, 2022, near Peter Pan Gulch Road and Olinda Road, just south of Anderson in Shasta County. The fire burned 304 acres and caused evacuations and power outages through the area, with 16 structures destroyed. Of the destroyed structures, 6 were residential and 10 were out buildings. 6 other buildings were also  damaged. No injuries were reported. The cause is under investigation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level jet diving down the coast of California, combined with plentiful lower level moisture advected from the Pacific set the stage for an early season rain storm and strong winds around much of the Bay Area and Central Coast. The highest winds occurred along the coast, where various sites reported gusts between 35 and 50 mph. A wind advisory was in effect from early Sunday morning through Sunday evening for the immediate coast and coastal hills. Highest rainfall totals were recorded in the coastal mountains from Sonoma County southward through Big Sur, resulting in localized flooding, downed trees/powerlines and debris/rocks sliding onto Highway 1 in Big Sur. A flood advisory was issued for a large portion of the Monterey Bay coastal zone and Big Sur coast from late night on the 18th through early morning on the 19th. Storm Total Rainfall measured 2 to 3.5 inches in North Bay Coastal Mountains, 1.5 to 2.2 inches in the Santa Cruz Mountains and 2 to 4.7 inches in the Santa Lucia's. Lower totals closer to an inch or less were recorded around the SF Bay Shoreline and coastal Monterey Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding due to heavy rain on Rt-17 offramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September 12 flash flood and debris and surveyed areas. Debris flow Oak Glen Potato Canyon Birch creek (caught on video time lapse) which as 100,000 cubic yards of debris. Debris flow Valley of the Falls on Prospect street (death) up to 8 feet high with large boulders. Debris flow on Valley of the Falls road (just up the road shown in survey) which was 10 feet with another 50,000 cubic yards of debris.  Debris flow San Gorgonio River (Apple scar) with the several million damage to the water plant in Banning. There were reports of damage and flooding in Crestline Lake Arrowhead (further west) from September 11 (3 inches rain) but never got photos.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major flash flood debris flow occurred in Birch Creek through Potato Canyon. This area was affected by lesser debris flows October and December 2021 and August 2022 but this flood filled the entire channel in layers, overtopped and damage the Steakhouse restaurant in Oak Glen, took out power poles, pushed large boulder and layers of mud downstream onto Oak Glen Road and pushed up to windows on a couple homes. WCM Alex Tardy did a storm survey. Estimated 100,000 cubic yards of debris removed by San Bernardino Public Works. Video of the debris flow between 3 and 4 pm was recorded.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SALINAS VALLEY / HOLLISTER VALLEY / CARMEL VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure encompassed the Western CONUS from September 1st through 8th, leading to anomalously hot temperatures along the California coast, and even more extreme temperatures in interior valleys and all mountain zones, where very hot daytime temperatures combined with a lack of overnight cooling created extreme heat risk . Excessive Heat Warnings were issued for interior areas and mountains of Monterey/San Benito Counties for the period September 1st through 8th, and for interior valleys and mountain zones around the San Francisco Bay Area from September 4th through 8th. A combination of Heat Advisories and an Excessive Heat Warning was issued for parts of Monterey Bay and its near coastal valleys, the San Francisco Bay Shoreline, and Marin Coastal Mountains from September 4th through 8th, along with a Heat Advisory for the Central Coast, San Francisco, and coastal North Bay on September 6th. Several daily record high temperature records were shattered, along with a handful of monthly and all-time records. Triple digit temperatures were reported across a vast swath of the interior, with some sites reaching 110 degrees and higher. Reports of power outages, heat related illnesses and deaths were received. Counties opened/operated one or more cooling centers to provide relief from the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hollister ASOS reported a max temperature of 106 deg. Carmel Valley coop site reported a max temperature 101 deg. Other unofficial sites in the vicinity reported max temperatures in the upper 90s to 104 deg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September 12 flash flood and debris and surveyed areas. Debris flow Oak Glen Potato Canyon Birch creek (caught on video time lapse) which as 100,000 cubic yards of debris. Debris flow Valley of the Falls on Prospect street (death) up to 8 feet high with large boulders. Debris flow on Valley of the Falls road (just up the road shown in survey) which was 10 feet with another 50,000 cubic yards of debris.  Debris flow San Gorgonio River (Apple scar) with the several million damage to the water plant in Banning. There were reports of damage and flooding in Crestline Lake Arrowhead (further west) from September 11 (3 inches rain) but never got photos.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large flash flood and debris flow occurred on Prospect street off of Valley of the Falls in Forest Falls, CA. The debris flow originated on the 2020 El Dorado burn scar after 1.1 inches of rain in an hour on Yucaipa Ridge. More than 1 debris flow occurred but the largest crossed Prospect street moving and damaging vehicles as well as damage homes. At least 2 homes had major damage. One fatality occurred when an older female was buried in debris, 3 days later found by search and rescue dogs. Residents took photos of debris flows and flash flooding including video and Ring security cameras. WCM visited the damage and SAR activity on September 13 and spoke with residents who shared additional photos and descriptions. A 20 foot boulder was lodged just above their home and their Jeep was washed down the hill (caught on Ring camera).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level jet diving down the coast of California, combined with plentiful lower level moisture advected from the Pacific set the stage for an early season rain storm and strong winds around much of the Bay Area and Central Coast. The highest winds occurred along the coast, where various sites reported gusts between 35 and 50 mph. A wind advisory was in effect from early Sunday morning through Sunday evening for the immediate coast and coastal hills. Highest rainfall totals were recorded in the coastal mountains from Sonoma County southward through Big Sur, resulting in localized flooding, downed trees/powerlines and debris/rocks sliding onto Highway 1 in Big Sur. A flood advisory was issued for a large portion of the Monterey Bay coastal zone and Big Sur coast from late night on the 18th through early morning on the 19th. Storm Total Rainfall measured 2 to 3.5 inches in North Bay Coastal Mountains, 1.5 to 2.2 inches in the Santa Cruz Mountains and 2 to 4.7 inches in the Santa Lucia's. Lower totals closer to an inch or less were recorded around the SF Bay Shoreline and coastal Monterey Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree collapsed near roadway, blocking lanes. Law enforcement closed both lanes on Highway 17 north of Scotts Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with Tropical Storm Kay blew down a newly planted tree on the campus of Sherman Indian High School in Riverside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure encompassed the Western CONUS from September 1st through 8th, leading to anomalously hot temperatures along the California coast, and even more extreme temperatures in interior valleys and all mountain zones, where very hot daytime temperatures combined with a lack of overnight cooling created extreme heat risk . Excessive Heat Warnings were issued for interior areas and mountains of Monterey/San Benito Counties for the period September 1st through 8th, and for interior valleys and mountain zones around the San Francisco Bay Area from September 4th through 8th. A combination of Heat Advisories and an Excessive Heat Warning was issued for parts of Monterey Bay and its near coastal valleys, the San Francisco Bay Shoreline, and Marin Coastal Mountains from September 4th through 8th, along with a Heat Advisory for the Central Coast, San Francisco, and coastal North Bay on September 6th. Several daily record high temperature records were shattered, along with a handful of monthly and all-time records. Triple digit temperatures were reported across a vast swath of the interior, with some sites reaching 110 degrees and higher. Reports of power outages, heat related illnesses and deaths were received. Counties opened/operated one or more cooling centers to provide relief from the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Santa Rosa Coop site set a new monthly max temperature record of 112 deg., breaking the previous record of 110 deg recorded in 2020, 2017, and 1971. Napa Coop site set a new daily max temperature record of 109 deg., breaking the previous record of 102 deg. set in 2020. Kentfield Coop site set a new daily record of 107 deg., breaking the previous record of 103 deg., set in 1950.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent moist monsoonal flow fueled thunderstorms over southeast California. Several storms produced flash flooding, and a few produced severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 95 was flooded just south of the CA/NV state line.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure strengthened over the area between June 9 and June 11.  This resulted in temperatures rising to well above normal readings by June 10 with some locations approaching record highs.  Heat Advisories were issued for the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Kern River Valley and an Excessive Heat Warning was issued for the Kern County Desert areas.  The heat continued on June 11 then a low pressure system moved into the Pacific Northwest on June 12 which pushed a cold front through the area resulting in temperatures cooling by a few degrees on June 12 and plunging to slightly below normal levels by June 13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 100 and 104 degrees on June 10 and June 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed ahead of an upper trough deepening off the Pacific Coast brought another round of strong winds area-wide, along with a chance for light showers and cooler temperatures to the region. Winds were the primary impact as thermal gradients tightened along the cold front with an accompanying increase in upper winds from a strong 100+ knot jet core that lifted north-northeast over the region. This setup increased confidence for moderate travel, recreation, and even fire weather impacts over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (SE445) located 2.6 mi S of Crowley Lake reported a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level jet diving down the coast of California, combined with plentiful lower level moisture advected from the Pacific set the stage for an early season rain storm and strong winds around much of the Bay Area and Central Coast. The highest winds occurred along the coast, where various sites reported gusts between 35 and 50 mph. A wind advisory was in effect from early Sunday morning through Sunday evening for the immediate coast and coastal hills. Highest rainfall totals were recorded in the coastal mountains from Sonoma County southward through Big Sur, resulting in localized flooding, downed trees/powerlines and debris/rocks sliding onto Highway 1 in Big Sur. A flood advisory was issued for a large portion of the Monterey Bay coastal zone and Big Sur coast from late night on the 18th through early morning on the 19th. Storm Total Rainfall measured 2 to 3.5 inches in North Bay Coastal Mountains, 1.5 to 2.2 inches in the Santa Cruz Mountains and 2 to 4.7 inches in the Santa Lucia's. Lower totals closer to an inch or less were recorded around the SF Bay Shoreline and coastal Monterey Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down blocking road at 3042 Soquel San Jose Rd.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure centered over the Great Basin between June 25 and June 27 provided for a period of much above normal temperatures across the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills, Sierra foothills and the Kern River Valley.  Daytime high temperatures generally ranged from 104 to 108 Degrees on each of these days.  The high began to weaken on June 28 and temperatures began to trend downward during the last few days of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 104 and 108 degrees between June 10 and June 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving through the Pacific Northwest pushed a cold front through the area between the evening of June 12 and the evening of June 14 resulting in a period of gusty winds along the Mojave Desert slopes in Kern County during this time frame.  The strongest winds were observed during the early afternoon of June 13 when law enforcement reported two tractor trailers were overturned as a result of the increased winds.  Several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Mojave Desert Slopes measured peak gusts between 60 mph and 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported an overturned tractor trailer along SR 14 near the Beacon Solar Plant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of high pressure strengthened over the area between June 9 and June 11.  This resulted in temperatures rising to well above normal readings by June 10 with some locations approaching record highs.  Heat Advisories were issued for the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Kern River Valley and an Excessive Heat Warning was issued for the Kern County Desert areas.  The heat continued on June 11 then a low pressure system moved into the Pacific Northwest on June 12 which pushed a cold front through the area resulting in temperatures cooling by a few degrees on June 12 and plunging to slightly below normal levels by June 13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daytime highs ranged between 100 and 104 degrees on June 10 and June 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorm activity occurred on June 21-22. Multiple small wildfires were started due to lightning strikes, and localized flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from associate meteorologist at San Diego County Flood Control District. ALERT rain gauge in Coyote Canyon recorded 0.58 inch in an hour (2-year return interval). Minor flooding reported around Montezuma Road in De Anza neighborhood of Borrego Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorm activity occurred on June 21-22. Multiple small wildfires were started due to lightning strikes, and localized flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of power outage to all of Borrego Springs around 4:05 pm, shortly after numerous lightning strikes in Palm Canyon and northwestern areas of Borrego Springs. Power remained off for 3+ hours and was restored between 7:00 pm and 7:30 pm. Several brief heavy downpours were also reported, with rainfall of 0.11 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated surf and strong rip currents led to hazardous swimming conditions in the middle of June. Mission Beach Lifeguards rescued two swimmers but a third was swept away, resulting in a drowning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mission Beach Lifeguards report the rescue of two swimmers struggling near a sand bar in elevated surf and strong rip currents. A third swimmer was swept away, resulting in a drowning fatality. The victim had reportedly saved his friend from a large wave and was swept away in a rip current.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog impacted areas around San Clemente Island the morning of June 21. Fog was so dense that there were issues allowing boats to get out of Wilson Cove when conducting military training exercises.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: LCDR Alex Edward with the United States Coast Guard reported dense fog off of San Clemente Island on June 22. Their ability to launch boats off of the island was inhibited by the dense fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving through the Pacific Northwest pushed a cold front through the area between the evening of June 12 and the evening of June 14 resulting in a period of gusty winds along the Mojave Desert slopes in Kern County during this time frame.  The strongest winds were observed during the early afternoon of June 13 when law enforcement reported two tractor trailers were overturned as a result of the increased winds.  Several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Mojave Desert Slopes measured peak gusts between 60 mph and 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison Station at Cottonwood Canyon measured a peak wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 2022 was yet another dry month across central California. Scattered showers and thunderstorms did impact the area between the 22nd and the 25th.  Otherwise dry weather prevailed across the area.  By the end of the month, nearly all of our area remained in an extreme or exceptional drought as most of our area has received less than a third of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Side Mountains north of Highway 198 were classified as being in an exceptional drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over interior northern California brought widespread moderate to locally high heat risk to the region. Triple digit temperatures were observed across much of the Valley on June 10th and 11th with the hottest temperatures on June 10th. A daily high temperature record was tied in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS reported high temperature readings ranged from 100 to 104 degrees during the event. The warmest high temperatures were observed on Friday the 10th and reached 104 degrees at the Marysville Airport. One cooling center was open through 8:00 pm in Yuba County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Rices Fire began when weather conditions were dry with relative humidity values around 13% and temperatures in the lower 90s. Southwest winds generally 5 to 8 mph with gusts up to 15 mph were observed with nearby ridge top gusts up to 20 mph. The fire experienced a moderate rate of spread.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rices Fire began on June 28, 2022, near Rices Crossing Road and Cranston Road, south of Dobbins in Nevada County. The fire burned 904 acres and caused evacuations through the area, with 1 structure destroyed. No injuries reported. The fire was not fully contained until 2:22 pm, July 11, 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Interior northern California is still experiencing widespread drought. No changes were made to drought severity levels for the month of June. Much of the region remains in severe to extreme drought with the southern portions of the region in exceptional drought. Many local and regional, as well as state-wide restrictions continue. Agricultural surface water deliveries are significantly reduced. The state-wide drought declaration by governor Gavin Newsom also continues. Mandatory water restrictions are in place on outdoor water use for all Californians with possible fines to violators. Agriculture and most of the state���s energy development are water-intensive industries and less water will mean communities across California are going to suffer this year during the drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the month of June, the U.S. Drought Monitor kept all of Zone 13 in extreme drought (D3). The zone saw drought impacts continue due to the below average precipitation for the water year at around 60% of average. Drought impacts include but are not limited too: agriculture, water supplies, recreation, and ecosystems.
