EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level jet diving down the coast of California, combined with plentiful lower level moisture advected from the Pacific set the stage for an early season rain storm and strong winds around much of the Bay Area and Central Coast. The highest winds occurred along the coast, where various sites reported gusts between 35 and 50 mph. A wind advisory was in effect from early Sunday morning through Sunday evening for the immediate coast and coastal hills. Highest rainfall totals were recorded in the coastal mountains from Sonoma County southward through Big Sur, resulting in localized flooding, downed trees/powerlines and debris/rocks sliding onto Highway 1 in Big Sur. A flood advisory was issued for a large portion of the Monterey Bay coastal zone and Big Sur coast from late night on the 18th through early morning on the 19th. Storm Total Rainfall measured 2 to 3.5 inches in North Bay Coastal Mountains, 1.5 to 2.2 inches in the Santa Cruz Mountains and 2 to 4.7 inches in the Santa Lucia's. Lower totals closer to an inch or less were recorded around the SF Bay Shoreline and coastal Monterey Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inch diameter branches blocking road at Bear Creek Rd and Huckleberry Ln.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of moisture from Tropical Storm Celia which was off the west coast of Mexico near 17N moved over a hot and unstable airmass which was situated over central California. This resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms over the area on June 22 which was also the first significant precipitation event for the area in two months.  Due to the convective nature of the precipitation, rainfall amounts varied; but a few thunderstorms produced one to two tenths of an inch of rainfall in the San Joaquin Valley.  Some stations in the Tehachapi Mountains and the south end of the upper Sierra picked up between a  quarter and three quarters of an inch of rainfall.  Some of the thunderstorms produced strong outflow winds and dry microbursts which resulted in several trees and power lines being downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E Station at Owens Mountain measured a peak wind gust of 69 mph from a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple days of excessive heat occurred across inland areas for several days June 9-13. Multiple wildfires were reported during this time frame, including fires near Wrightwood, Oceanside and along the US-Mexico border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheep wildfire started at 6:45 am on June 12 near the intersection of Highway 2 and Sheep Creek Road east of Wrightwood. It moved quickly uphill. A second fire broke out at 8:07 pm, forcing a hard road closure at Highway 2 and Wright Mountain Road. The fire was contained at 2:02 pm on June 19. The fire burned 865 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure encompassed the Western CONUS from September 1st through 8th, leading to anomalously hot temperatures along the California coast, and even more extreme temperatures in interior valleys and all mountain zones, where very hot daytime temperatures combined with a lack of overnight cooling created extreme heat risk . Excessive Heat Warnings were issued for interior areas and mountains of Monterey/San Benito Counties for the period September 1st through 8th, and for interior valleys and mountain zones around the San Francisco Bay Area from September 4th through 8th. A combination of Heat Advisories and an Excessive Heat Warning was issued for parts of Monterey Bay and its near coastal valleys, the San Francisco Bay Shoreline, and Marin Coastal Mountains from September 4th through 8th, along with a Heat Advisory for the Central Coast, San Francisco, and coastal North Bay on September 6th. Several daily record high temperature records were shattered, along with a handful of monthly and all-time records. Triple digit temperatures were reported across a vast swath of the interior, with some sites reaching 110 degrees and higher. Reports of power outages, heat related illnesses and deaths were received. Counties opened/operated one or more cooling centers to provide relief from the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Redwood City tied its all-time record high temperature of 110 deg set in 1972. It also beat its monthly record 108 deg set in 2020.

By the end of the month, nearly all of our area remained in an extreme or exceptional drought as most of our area has received less than a third of normal precipitation since July 1, 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grapevine area was classified as being in an exceptional drought due to a long term precipitation deficit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 590 DAM ridge at 500 mb built over CA Thursday June 9 into Friday June 10, leading to rapid warming of the airmass and daytime surface temperatures reaching into the 90s to over 100 degrees over a large swath of the state away from the coast. A heat advisory was issued covering interior portions (mountains and valleys) of the SF Bay Area and Central Coast for June 10 from 11 AM until 10 PM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sonoma County Airport reached a high of 102 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level troughing brought seasonably wet weather with thunderstorms to interior Northern California. A debris flow due to heavy rain was observed in the Dixie Fire burn scar on Hwy 70 in the northern Sierra as well as a record daily rainfall report of 0.47 inches that was set at the Red Bluff Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Plumas County Sheriff reported that there were two rock/mud slides and/or debris flows on Hwy 70 one mile west of Belden and near Rogers Flat in the Dixie Fire Burn Scar, with vehicles trapped between the slides. The occupants were rescued, however their vehicles remained temporarily stranded in the area with the road closed for several days. Rain gauge reports from 0.50 to 1.00 inches over a 2 hour period from 12 pm to 2 pm.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SALINAS VALLEY / ARROYO SECO / LAKE SAN ANTONIO
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure encompassed the Western CONUS from September 1st through 8th, leading to anomalously hot temperatures along the California coast, and even more extreme temperatures in interior valleys and all mountain zones, where very hot daytime temperatures combined with a lack of overnight cooling created extreme heat risk . Excessive Heat Warnings were issued for interior areas and mountains of Monterey/San Benito Counties for the period September 1st through 8th, and for interior valleys and mountain zones around the San Francisco Bay Area from September 4th through 8th. A combination of Heat Advisories and an Excessive Heat Warning was issued for parts of Monterey Bay and its near coastal valleys, the San Francisco Bay Shoreline, and Marin Coastal Mountains from September 4th through 8th, along with a Heat Advisory for the Central Coast, San Francisco, and coastal North Bay on September 6th. Several daily record high temperature records were shattered, along with a handful of monthly and all-time records. Triple digit temperatures were reported across a vast swath of the interior, with some sites reaching 110 degrees and higher. Reports of power outages, heat related illnesses and deaths were received. Counties opened/operated one or more cooling centers to provide relief from the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: King City reported an all-time record high temperature of 116 deg, beating the previous record 115 deg set in 2017.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cool air mass combined with clearing skies to bring freezing temperatures to northeast California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The only reported low temperature in this zone was 29 degrees at the Alturas ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front with an anonymously deep upper trough brought another round of windy conditions, increased chances for showers and thunderstorms, and cooler temperatures that dampened summer recreation activities and made travel difficult across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH03) Mammoth Mountain located 1.7 mi S of the Mammoth Mountain Ski Summit, CA reported a peak wind gust of 112 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably deep trough of low pressure brought isolated high wind to the Owens Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak measured gust occurred 4 miles NW of Independence. Strong winds in Bishop blew down a power line and a tree branch, and also ripped the metal roof off a shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure brought very hot and dry conditions to northern California on these days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed temperatures in this zone were sparse.  The cooperative observer at Happy Camp reported a high temperature of 111 degrees on 09/06/22.  On 09/07/22, the same observer reported a low temperature of 59 degrees and a high temperatures of 107 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front with an anonymously deep upper trough brought another round of windy conditions, increased chances for showers and thunderstorms, and cooler temperatures that dampened summer recreation activities and made travel difficult across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (HVRTM) Blue Streak Weather station located 1.0 mi NNW of the Heavenly Ski Summit, CA reported a peak wind gust of 88 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (DYLC1-DOYLE) located 3.0 mi N of Doyle, CA reported a peak gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold low pressure approaching from the northwest brought isolated high downslope wind to the Owens Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: These gusts occurred 4 miles NW of Independence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Aspendell measured 24.5 inches of snow.

CZ_NAME: SANTA CLARA VALLEY INCLUDING SAN JOSE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent cold pattern caused several mornings of near and sub-freezing temperatures around the Bay Area and Central Coast. This was the second major cold event of the season and the most widespread. Freeze Warnings were issued for the North Bay interior valleys from Dec 12-16, other interior valley and mountain zones from Dec 13-16, with Frost Advisories for coastal zones Dec 13-16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six unhoused residents died from cold-related illness between the 13th and 14th. Four of those were in San Jose aged 40s-70s, with two others in unspecified areas in Santa Clara county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river impacted the Bay Area on December 31st, resulting in significant rainfall across the San Francisco Bay Area. Initial forecasts called for the most intense rainband to progressively move south towards the Central Coast as the day went on. However, the morning of the 31st, a surface low developed west of San Francisco, and the river stalled over the Bay Area. This resulted in significant rainfall totals for many Bay Area sites. Most notably, downtown San Francisco received 5.46 inches of rain on the 31st, the second wettest day on record. The Oakland Museum received its wettest day since records began in 1970, with 4.75 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Redwood City with 4.47. ||12/31/2022 was the 3rd wettest in a record that goes back to 1906. The daily rainfall made up 42.8% of December 2022's rainfall.||The wettest day on record at Redwood City was back on 10/13/1962 with 4.88 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level jet diving down the coast of California, combined with plentiful lower level moisture advected from the Pacific set the stage for an early season rain storm and strong winds around much of the Bay Area and Central Coast. The highest winds occurred along the coast, where various sites reported gusts between 35 and 50 mph. A wind advisory was in effect from early Sunday morning through Sunday evening for the immediate coast and coastal hills. Highest rainfall totals were recorded in the coastal mountains from Sonoma County southward through Big Sur, resulting in localized flooding, downed trees/powerlines and debris/rocks sliding onto Highway 1 in Big Sur. A flood advisory was issued for a large portion of the Monterey Bay coastal zone and Big Sur coast from late night on the 18th through early morning on the 19th. Storm Total Rainfall measured 2 to 3.5 inches in North Bay Coastal Mountains, 1.5 to 2.2 inches in the Santa Cruz Mountains and 2 to 4.7 inches in the Santa Lucia's. Lower totals closer to an inch or less were recorded around the SF Bay Shoreline and coastal Monterey Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gust 44 mph measured by PG&E Mill Creek Vineyard weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper disturbance moved east across Northwest California during early to mid December. Locally heavy low-elevation rainfall was observed, along with areas of heavy mountain snow. Impacts due to falling trees occurring with periods of strong gusty winds were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused rock and mud to flow across Highway 101.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving down the Pacific Coast tapped into sub-tropical moisture, bringing heavy rain and strong winds from the night of December 26th through the 27th. A wind advisory was issued for the immediate coastline and coastal mountains and East Bay Hills. A Flood Watch covered the same coastal areas and the North Bay valleys, and several Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories were issued for areas under the Watch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto power lines near the intersection of Moraga and Rimrock Roads, causing Moraga Road to be closed from Sky Hy Drive (Yes, that spelling is correct.) and St Mary's Road in Lafayette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed through the San Francisco Bay Area and dropped brief, heavy rain on the morning of December 1st. That night, skies cleared, winds settled down, and temperatures plummeted. Freeze warnings were issued for interior North Bay, the hills of South Bay, and interior parts of the Central Coast. Temperatures were well below normal region-wide, reaching the mid and upper 20s Fahrenheit in the coldest interior spots, and 30s along the coast and Bay Shoreline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record daily low temperature, 36 deg F, reached in downtown Oakland (museum). The previous record was 38 deg F set in 1973.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level jet diving down the coast of California, combined with plentiful lower level moisture advected from the Pacific set the stage for an early season rain storm and strong winds around much of the Bay Area and Central Coast. The highest winds occurred along the coast, where various sites reported gusts between 35 and 50 mph. A wind advisory was in effect from early Sunday morning through Sunday evening for the immediate coast and coastal hills. Highest rainfall totals were recorded in the coastal mountains from Sonoma County southward through Big Sur, resulting in localized flooding, downed trees/powerlines and debris/rocks sliding onto Highway 1 in Big Sur. A flood advisory was issued for a large portion of the Monterey Bay coastal zone and Big Sur coast from late night on the 18th through early morning on the 19th. Storm Total Rainfall measured 2 to 3.5 inches in North Bay Coastal Mountains, 1.5 to 2.2 inches in the Santa Cruz Mountains and 2 to 4.7 inches in the Santa Lucia's. Lower totals closer to an inch or less were recorded around the SF Bay Shoreline and coastal Monterey Bay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gust 46 mph measured by PG&E Stewarts Point weather station on Highway 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought isolated high winds and isolated flooding from heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chollita Rd was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad east to southeast flow advected in monsoon moisture, which fueled thunderstorms over southeast California. Several storms produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hwy 190 was completely impassable east of Panamint Valley Rd due to a large flow of water, debris, and rocks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep-layer westerly flow occurring along a zonally oriented quasi-stationary frontal boundary yielded a multi-day atmospheric river event across Northwest California during late December. Widespread impacts due to heavy rain and strong winds were observed across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a landslide that blocked one lane of Redwood Drive between Redway and Garberville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Twenty miles south of Emigrant Gap, CA. Timber, brush and hardwood litter. Extreme fire behavior with running, spotting and torching. Numerous structures threatened. Evacuations, road, area and trail closures were in effect. 78 structures were lost to the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense smoke from the Mosquito Fire impacted the Tahoe Basin and Truckee areas much of this week, with periods of hazardous air quality and low visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep layer of monsoonal moisture pushed northwest and north into California, Sierra, and western Nevada in a southerly upper flow that marked the western margins of an area of strong high-pressure ridging into the Great Basin region.  Thickening mid-level moisture coupled with steep afternoon lapse rates and warmer surface heating initiated deep columns of slow-moving moisture-laden showers and thunderstorms with torrential rains over the 2021 Tamarack burn scar. This resulted in widespread flash flooding and debris flows in Alpine County CA that created a number of impacts. Rainfall rates of 2.22 inches per hour were observed during the peak of the storm in Markleeville, CA which corresponds to at least a 500 year recurrence interval and possibly 1000 year, per NOAA 14 atlas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe flooding was reported along State Route 89 in and out of Markleeville, CA with numerous debris flows. Roadway damage was also reported, along with reports of vehicles starting to get swept away.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the vicinity of the Tamarack Fire Burn Scar caused a slide over State Route 4 that was reported by CHP to be 3-4 feet deep and closed the highway in both directions. No rainfall reports in the exact location, but estimates of 0.5-1 were reported to the south and 2-3 to the north, which occurred in a few hours. This is in addition to the heavy rainfall the day before.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another weekend storm delivered widespread rain, mountain snow and gusty winds to interior NorCal. Localized flooding, downed trees, and mountain travel impacts were observed starting on 12/9/22 through early 12/12/22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river impacted the Bay Area on December 31st, resulting in significant rainfall across the San Francisco Bay Area. Initial forecasts called for the most intense rainband to progressively move south towards the Central Coast as the day went on. However, the morning of the 31st, a surface low developed west of San Francisco, and the river stalled over the Bay Area. This resulted in significant rainfall totals for many Bay Area sites. Most notably, downtown San Francisco received 5.46 inches of rain on the 31st, the second wettest day on record. The Oakland Museum received its wettest day since records began in 1970, with 4.75 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CHP reported a car stuck in water, blocking the offramp from Southbound Interstate 280 to Monterey Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving down the Pacific Coast tapped into sub-tropical moisture, bringing heavy rain and strong winds from the night of December 26th through the 27th. A wind advisory was issued for the immediate coastline and coastal mountains and East Bay Hills. A Flood Watch covered the same coastal areas and the North Bay valleys, and several Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories were issued for areas under the Watch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 70 mph gust was recorded at the PGE weather station at Wolfback Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger winter storm brought another round of heavy snow, strong winds, and travel impacts to Sierra passes 9-11 December. Peak snowfall totals occurred through the period of 10-11 December with 2-4 feet of snow widely reported in Sierra locations and northeast CA.  Storm total snowfall amounts even exceeded 5 feet over parts of the Sierra crest. Several inches of new snow also fell over some western Nevada valleys with a foot plus falling across some eastern Sierra foothill areas as well.  Strong winds having gusts 65-75 mph in far western NV resulted in reports of damage across parts of Sparks during the early morning of 10 Dec.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site (HVRTM) Blue Streak Weather station located 1.0 mi NNW of the Heavenly Ski Summit, CA reported a peak wind gust of 103 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strongest winter storm of December passed through the region on the final two days of December. This storm initially produced very high snow levels near 9000 feet on 30 December.  Colder air and enhanced forcing, coupled with the final push of extensive moisture worked in unison to rapidly drop snow levels to most valley floors (above 4000 feet) during the morning of New Year's Eve. Although this early change-over to snow reduced the flooding risks, it brought widespread winter travel hazards region-wide. Storm total snow totals ranged from 5��� to around 1 foot for lower elevations of western NV/northeast CA with lesser amounts below 4000 feet in west-central Nevada and areas near the Oregon border. Some foothill areas received 1-2 feet plus with Sierra snowfall totals generally ranging from 18-30 inches below 7000 feet. Higher Sierra areas and passes received 3-4 feet plus above 7000 feet. During the final week of December, a very active weather set up with a series of generally warmer Pacific winter storms moving through the region. These winter storms were able to tap into a deep layer of subtropical moisture provided by attendant atmospheric rivers that pushed snow levels higher as compared to earlier storm systems.  The initial winter storm from 26-27 December pushed snow levels mainly above 7000 feet where 1-2 feet of snow fell.  Sierra locations below 7000 feet generally received only 1-6 inches of accumulated snow. Precipitation across much of the rest of western Nevada fell as rain with 0.50-1 inch rainfall totals measured around the Reno-Sparks-Carson City multiplex, which continued south into Douglas and Lyon counties. Lesser precipitation amounts were reported farther east. Strong wind gusts were also reported on the 27 and 30 December, especially in Mineral, Lyon, and southern Churchill counties where weather spotters and the public reported some tree damage. Wind gusts over 90 mph near Walker Lake blew over several tractor-trailers along US-95 while peak gusts across Sierra ridge-tops exceeded 150 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (DYLC1-DOYLE) located 3.0 mi N of Doyle, CA reported a peak gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger winter storm brought another round of heavy snow, strong winds, and travel impacts to Sierra passes 9-11 December. Peak snowfall totals occurred through the period of 10-11 December with 2-4 feet of snow widely reported in Sierra locations and northeast CA.  Storm total snowfall amounts even exceeded 5 feet over parts of the Sierra crest. Several inches of new snow also fell over some western Nevada valleys with a foot plus falling across some eastern Sierra foothill areas as well.  Strong winds having gusts 65-75 mph in far western NV resulted in reports of damage across parts of Sparks during the early morning of 10 Dec.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 2SSW of Weed reported 11 inches of snow in 6 hours, ending at 01/0300 PST.  The event crossed the monthly boundary. An estimated 5.5 inches fell after midnight.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Indian Well reported a peak gust of 69 mph on 12/26 at 2301.  Van Bremmer reported a peak gust of 67 mph on 12/26 at 2031.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of abundant boundary layer moisture, clearing skies, and nearly calm winds led to the development of dense fog across portions of I-8 in far southeast Imperial County during the morning of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility as low as 300 feet reported in spots along I-8 between Yuma, AZ and Holtville, CA from dense fog. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first deep, cold trough of the season brought locally severe downslope winds in the lee of the Sierra Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust occurred 4 miles NW of Independence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure to the southeast wrapped in enough moisture to fuel isolated thunderstorms over southern San Bernardino County. One storm produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yucca Creek flooded Indio Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cut-off low pressure system near the Baja Peninsula pulled moisture north into San Bernardino County, and difluence aloft helped trigger thunderstorms. A few storms produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flowed down Utah Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cut-off low pressure system near the Baja Peninsula pulled moisture north into San Bernardino County, and difluence aloft helped trigger thunderstorms. A few storms produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large rocks and boulders washed onto Amboy Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide covered Parker Dam Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: About two feet of water and debris flowed over Amboy Rd at Midway Rd. At least one vehicle was stuck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep-layer westerly flow occurring along a zonally oriented quasi-stationary frontal boundary yielded a multi-day atmospheric river event across Northwest California during late December. Widespread impacts due to heavy rain and strong winds were observed across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 20 flooded near Pingree Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep-layer westerly flow occurring along a zonally oriented quasi-stationary frontal boundary yielded a multi-day atmospheric river event across Northwest California during late December. Widespread impacts due to heavy rain and strong winds were observed across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused rock and mud to slide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper disturbance moved east across Northwest California during early to mid December. Locally heavy low-elevation rainfall was observed, along with areas of heavy mountain snow. Impacts due to falling trees occurring with periods of strong gusty winds were also observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a rockslide that spread across Highway 299 leading to a truck accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High instability (MUCAPE 2,000 J/kg) and abundant moisture (PWATs 1.8-2.0) created an environment conducive to strong to severe thunderstorm development along terrain features in southeast California during the afternoon of the 16th. DCAPE values around 1,200 J/kg supported strong to severe downburst winds. Thunderstorms initiated over Joshua Tree National Park during the early afternoon hours and produced flash flooding in the south end of the park. Four semi-trucks were also blown over on I-10 near Chiriaco Summit. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several mobile homes in Chiriaco Summit sustained roof damage. A nearby gas station also sustained damage to its canopy. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough combined with strong instability (SB CAPE of 2000+ J/KG) and high moisture content (PWAT of 1.7+ inches) to result in the development of strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of Riverside and Imperial Counties during the afternoon and early evening hours. A complex of thunderstorms originating from southwest Arizona moved westward through Riverside while intensifying in the process. Another complex of storms then developed near the international border and affected Imperial County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Boundary Road of Joshua Tree National Park was closed due to flooding. All visitors were evacuated from the southern portion the park. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist (PWATs 2.0+ inches) and unstable (MLCAPE 2000-3000 J/KG) air mass led to a very conducive environment for vigorous thunderstorm activity across portions of southeast California. Thunderstorms first developed across the higher terrain near Joshua Tree National Park as well as across the mountains of Northern Baja California. With the upper-level flow from the west, these thunderstorms moved eastward into Imperial and Riverside counties, moving into the very moist and unstable air mass. Radar estimated rainfall amounts were upwards of 2-3 inches in some areas, with most of the rain falling in less than 2 hours. There were several incidences of flash flooding and wind impacts around the region. The biggest impact of the day was a washout of a section of eastbound lanes along Interstate-10, which is a major throughway for ground traffic between Phoenix, AZ and Los Angeles, CA. This caused major traffic delays and detours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: S22(Borrego-Salton roadway) was closed due to flooding. Timing estimated from radar. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough combined with strong instability (SB CAPE of 2000+ J/KG) and high moisture content (PWAT of 1.7+ inches) to result in the development of strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of Riverside and Imperial Counties during the afternoon and early evening hours. A complex of thunderstorms originating from southwest Arizona moved westward through Riverside while intensifying in the process. Another complex of storms then developed near the international border and affected Imperial County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pinto Basin Road in Joshua Tree National Park was closed due to washes running over road. The water was estimated to be running 1 foot in depth over the road. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist (PWATs 2.0+ inches) and unstable (MLCAPE 2000-3000 J/KG) air mass led to a very conducive environment for vigorous thunderstorm activity across portions of southeast California. Thunderstorms first developed across the higher terrain near Joshua Tree National Park as well as across the mountains of Northern Baja California. With the upper-level flow from the west, these thunderstorms moved eastward into Imperial and Riverside counties, moving into the very moist and unstable air mass. Radar estimated rainfall amounts were upwards of 2-3 inches in some areas, with most of the rain falling in less than 2 hours. There were several incidences of flash flooding and wind impacts around the region. The biggest impact of the day was a washout of a section of eastbound lanes along Interstate-10, which is a major throughway for ground traffic between Phoenix, AZ and Los Angeles, CA. This caused major traffic delays and detours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caltrans closed SR-177 in both directions between I-10 and SR-62 due to flooding in multiple locations. Power lines were also down along SR-177. SR-177 was reopened around 0800PST, August 25th. Timing of flooding impacts based on radar. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A park employee with Joshua Tree National Park share video and report of roads washed out in the Indian Cove section of the park. No damages or injuries were reported. Timing based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed near Desert Center during the afternoon hours on the 28th as daytime heating combined orographic forcing aided in convective development. MLCAPE values upwards of 2,000-2,500 J/kg allowed these thunderstorms to become strong to severe. Adequate surface vorticity in place was enough for a landspout tornado to develop in Desert Center. Weak steering flow allowed for this landspout to remain over an open area with little movement. No damage or injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images and videos showed a nearly stationary landspout over an open area north of Lake Tamarisk Desert Resort in Desert Center. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized complex of strong to severe thunderstorms pushed from northwest Arizona down through the Lower Colorado River Valley area late in the night on the 21st and into the early morning hours on the 22nd. Thunderstorms maintained their intensity as they pushed through far eastern Riverside County, with adequate instability in place. Severe wind gusts and dense blowing dust were observed in Blythe with these thunderstorms. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blythe Airport (KBLH) recorded a 58 mph gust. At 2335PDT, a 63 mph gust was recorded at KBLH with the leading edge of an outflow boundary racing ahead of the parent thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abundance of moisture (PWAT 1.5+) and moderate to high instability (MLCAPE of 1000-2000 J/KG) led to the development of terrain and diurnally induced thunderstorm activity across portions of southwest Arizona during the afternoon hours. Some of this activity spread westward across the Lower Colorado River Valley towards extreme eastern Riverside County, where a slowing moving thunderstorm led to flash flooding and debris flow along Highway 95 north of Blythe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water, rocks, and debris were reported across Hwy 95 north of Blythe. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate-10, a major throughway between Phoenix, AZ and Southern California, had a section of the two eastbound (EB) lanes fully washed out due to flash flooding. Video during the washout appear to show the drainage pipes under the road were overcome and compromised by the magnitude of floodwaters. Radar rainfall estimates in this area were around 2-3 inches, with much of the rain falling in less than 2 hours. The washout occurred a few miles east of State Route 177/Desert Center. Caltrans closed I-10 EB due to the washout. I-10 westbound (WB) was also closed briefly due to an overturned semi-truck and SR-177 had already been closed, due to its own separate flooding impacts and downed power lines, by the time of the I-10 washout. This caused major traffic delays and detours through Eastern Riverside County. One EB detour lane was opened around 0700PST on August 25th after emergency construction and the second EB detour lane was not opened until 2300PST August 27th. Timing of flooding impacts based on radar. No injuries were reported from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Kay moving northward just west of Baja California pushed deep tropical moisture towards southern CA, where objective analysis showed PWATs in excess of 2 inches. Bands of moderate to heavy rainfall associated with the outer bands from the tropical storm affected most of Imperial County, resulting in significant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several incidents occurred between the Mountain Springs and Ocotillo area due to flash flooding. Large rocks were reported falling onto I-8. Significant water flow was reported along Meyer Creek wash, resulting in several roads which intersect the wash to become impassable. Highway 98 was closed between Imperial Highway and Drew Road due to the flooding. A mesonet station in Mountain Spring reported 2.67 inches with 1.02 inches falling in one hour. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under deep-layer easterly wind flow, subtle disturbances in the upper-levels helped to instigate thunderstorm activity across portions of south-central and southwestern Arizona during the late afternoon and early evening hours, which then made its way across the Lower Colorado River Valley to affect the town of Winterhaven in extreme Southeast Imperial County. The town was hit with damaging winds in form of dozens of downed power poles, leading to several hundred customers losing power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Imperial Irrigation District reported 68 power poles downed that left 351 people without power between the Winterhaven and Bard area. Timing estimated from radar. Winds were estimated between 65-70 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough situated just off the California Coast resulted in an influx of moisture into southeast California. Showers and thunderstorms developed in the afternoon hours across the eastern half of Riverside County with a northward motion. Heavy rainfall, which resulted in flash flooding just north of Desert Center damaging a law enforcement vehicle, as well as blowing dust from outward propagating outflows were the primarily hazards from these storms. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media video showed roughly 1/4 mile visibility in blowing dust from a thunderstorm outflow along I-10, west of Blythe. No accidents or injuries were reported.
